What is supply chain logistics?
Diverse Team Working Together in Modern Co-Working Space
Authors
Staff Writer
IBM Think
Staff Editor
IBM Think
What is supply chain logistics?
Supply chain logistics refers to the management of the production, transportation and distribution of goods throughout a business’ overall supply chain. It encompasses both inbound logistics (how materials and supplies are brought into a business) and outbound logistics (how the business’ resulting products get to retailers and consumers).
Supply chain logistics plays a vital role in the smooth functioning of businesses and the global economy, supporting the flow of goods worldwide. Implementing effective logistics can help companies build resilience amid global volatility and gain a competitive advantage.
Logistics management involves coordinating activities tied to the sourcing, procurement and movement of raw materials to factories; transforming these materials into finished products; and delivering products to retailers and customers. It includes a range of issues, such as inventory management, warehousing, transportation and distribution, in addition to customer service and returns management.
Industry newsletter
The latest tech news, backed by expert insights
Stay up to date on the most important—and intriguing—industry trends on AI, automation, data and beyond with the Think Newsletter. See the IBM Privacy Statement.
Thank you! You are subscribed.
Your subscription will be delivered in English. You will find an unsubscribe link in every newsletter. You can manage your subscriptions or unsubscribe here. Refer to our IBM Privacy Statement for more information.
Why is supply chain logistics important?
Logistics is critical to getting goods to consumers as quickly and easily as possible. Effective supply chain logistics management helps reduce costs and improve operational efficiency across business processes. By optimizing each part of the supply chain, including demand planning, transportation routes, inventory levels and lead times, companies can save money and time.
Logistics can also help maintain customer satisfaction by ensuring customers receive their orders when and where they expect them. Together, these benefits can help drive a competitive advantage. Well-managed logistics allow companies to respond quickly to market changes, offer better delivery terms and address disruption in the most cost-effective ways.
A well-managed supply chain network benefits businesses, consumers and other stakeholders. For customers, efficient logistics means timely deliveries and accurate order fulfillment, all leading to a positive shopping experience. Customers also benefit from well managed “reverse logistics," or the movement of goods from customers back to their point of origin, either as retail returns or for recycling and disposal.
Well-managed logistics can also be a boon to retailers and wholesalers. Efficient inventory management reduces holding costs and effective transportation management cuts shipping expenses, improving overall profitability.
Become an AI expert
Gain the knowledge to prioritize AI investments that drive business growth. Get started with our free AI Academy today and lead the future of AI in your organization.
What is supply chain management?
Supply chain management (SCM) is a broader term that includes supply chain logistics as one of its elements. SCM involves overseeing the entire lifecycle of a product throughout the supply chain process, from the initial sourcing of materials to the final delivery of finished goods to consumers. It goes beyond logistical activities to include procurement strategy, product development, production management and demand forecasting.
As a part of SCM, supply chain risk management (SCRM) is the process of finding and addressing potential vulnerabilities in a company’s supply chain. SCRM aims to minimize the impact of these risks on a company's supply chain operations, its reputation and financial performance.
What technology does supply chain logistics use?
Many companies are looking for ways to better secure the movement of goods across the global supply chain to ensure resiliency amid disruption. But from production planning to the storage of goods to resolving problems for end customers, logistics is a complex feat.
Coordinating supply chain management activities across multiple suppliers, manufacturers and transportation providers working in different regions adds complexity to planning and organization. Additionally, customer demand is ever-changing, which means to adapt to rapidly changing market trends and customer needs can be difficult. Identifying and mitigating risks, including supplier reliability, transportation disruptions and geopolitical factors, leads to challenges.
Some companies use technology to help with the challenges of supply chain management. Advanced technologies offer new and powerful ways to work with supply chain analytics and improve supply chain visibility and transparency, including:
Sensors, GPS and Internet of Things (IoT) devices can provide a wealth of real-time information on the location and condition of goods at every stage of the supply chain. Supply chain data collected from various points can drive optimization by providing insights into operational efficiencies, potential risks and areas for improvement.
Automation technologies in warehouses, such as automated storage and retrieval systems (ASRS) and robotic picking systems, have significantly increased efficiency. They reduce manual labor, minimize errors and speed up the process of sorting, storing and retrieving goods. In transportation logistics, automated systems optimize load planning and vehicle scheduling, enhancing the efficiency of shipping operations.
As an immutable, accessible ledger, blockchain can enhance transparency and traceability across the supply chain, making it easier to verify the authenticity of products and track the movement of goods.
Artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) algorithms can analyze vast amounts of data to identify the most efficient transportation routes, spotlight potential disruptions or inconsistencies and gain insight into environmental impact. Simulation software can help with modeling potential scenarios to predict risk and develop contingency plans, making logistics operations more proactive and less reactive.
Cloud computing can provide scalable solutions for data storage and sharing, information systems and other components that make it easier to collaborate and exchange information at every stage of the supply chain.
What roles are involved in supply chain logistics?
Several key roles contribute to the smooth operation of supply chain logistics. Logistics managers oversee logistics operations, including transportation and warehousing and help align decisions with broader company goals. Procurement specialists manage purchasing of raw materials and goods, while transportation operations managers and warehouse managers are responsible for maintaining efficiency in their respective areas.
Inventory planners maintain the right levels of stock and customer service representatives take order inquiries and manage returns or problems. Most supply chain professionals hold multiple roles1 in the span of their careers. For logistics-specific work, they often seek certificates or bachelor’s degrees that specialize in the field.
How does supply chain logistics affect sustainability?
More companies are seeking ways to reduce their environmental impact, by limiting greenhouse gas emissions, pursuing decarbonization and setting net-zero goals. Supply chain sustainability plays a critical role in reaching these goals.
The Council of Supply Chain Management Professionals (CSCMP) defines supply chain sustainability as the management of environmental and social impacts within and across networks consisting of suppliers, manufacturers, distributors and customers in line with the UN Sustainable Development Goals.2 These efforts span every phase of the supply chain, from raw material sourcing and extraction to product use and the end of the product's life.
Effective logistics planning is an important part of supply chain sustainability. For example, optimizing transportation and distribution routes and using eco-friendly vehicles may help reduce fuel usage and overall emissions across the supply chain. Supply chain logistics can also help companies promote circular economy principles—by reducing waste and aiding recycling efforts—and identify areas for improvement in manufacturing processes.
Advanced technologies like AI and supply chain data analytics allow logistics teams to coordinate effective, responsive networks that adapt quickly to changes, avoiding overproduction and excess inventory that can undermine sustainability efforts. Through well-managed logistics, companies can operate through a more sustainable business model and contribute to a healthier planet.

The supply chain encompasses every stage of the production and logistics process
Supply chain: What is it, and how do you design one for your business?
The supply chain covers all the processes required to ensure products reach end customers, from raw material sourcing to delivery. In this post, we explore what a supply chain is, its key components and characteristics, and how to optimize it to improve operational efficiency.
What is a supply chain?
A supply chain is the set of processes, resources, and stakeholders involved in producing and distributing products. It includes all the activities required to turn raw materials into finished goods and deliver them to customers.
An efficient supply chain keeps costs down, enables a quick response to market demands, and enhances customer satisfaction. Core functions include demand planning, inventory management, storage, transportation, and distribution.
What’s the difference between logistics and the supply chain?
Although often used interchangeably, logistics and supply chain are distinct concepts with different scopes and purposes.
Logistics refers to the set of operations carried out to move, store, and distribute goods within a company or between specific points. It comprises activities such as inventory management, order fulfillment, transportation, and last-mile delivery.
The supply chain, on the other hand, has a broader scope. It encompasses all the stages required to manufacture and distribute products — from sourcing raw materials to delivering finished goods to end customers. This includes not only logistics but also demand planning, supplier relations, manufacturing, and reverse logistics (returns, recycling, etc.).
In short, logistics is the part of the supply chain responsible for carrying out day-to-day operations and one of its key links.
What are the stages of the supply chain?
A company’s supply chain is typically broken down into five main stages. Each must be carefully coordinated to avoid supply chain issues.
Characteristics of an efficient supply chain
While there are different types of supply chains, all should share common traits to run smoothly. These are the most crucial:
These elements are critical for building a responsive, high-performing supply chain that meets market demands.
A supply chain should be efficient, timely, cost-effective, and deliver a high level of service
A supply chain should be efficient, timely, cost-effective, and deliver a high level of service
How do companies design their supply chains?
Designing an effective supply chain involves planning and coordinating every element that affects the flow of products, information, and resources. Below are the steps to building a strong supply chain.
Designing a supply chain isn’t a one-off effort: it should evolve to accommodate changing business needs, new technologies, and market dynamics.
Digitalization provides full visibility across the entire supply chain
Digitalization provides full visibility across the entire supply chain
Supply chain examples
Supply chains vary by industry. However, they all share the same goal: to deliver the right product, at the right time, at the lowest possible cost. Below are real-world supply chain examples from different industries, featuring warehouses equipped and managed with Easy WMS software:
Automotive – Continental (Mexico)
In the automotive industry, timing is everything. Continental completed its supply chain optimization project in Mexico by implementing a new automated warehouse designed by Mecalux and overseen by Easy WMS. This solution enables the company to store over 4,000 pallets and prepare up to 170 orders daily, ensuring on-time supplies of components to production lines.
Food – Rica Group (Dominican Republic)
Cold chain integrity is paramount in much of the food supply chain. Rica Group bolstered its supply chain with an automated storage and retrieval system (AS/RS) with a capacity for over 10,000 refrigerated pallets. Mecalux’s solution provides safe, efficient storage of juices and dairy products while consistently maintaining quality.
Pharmaceutical – Intersurgical (Lithuania)
Pharma logistics calls for traceability and control. Intersurgical implemented an automated warehouse in Lithuania to optimize the storage of medical supplies such as masks and respirators. Mecalux’s system streamlines internal flows and ensures high-priority products are available for distribution.
The supply chain: A key factor in competitiveness
The supply chain plays a central role in helping companies stay ahead of the curve. Effective management minimizes costs, optimizes resources, and improves customer service. In a fast-changing global environment, having a well-designed, technology-driven supply chain is more important than ever.
The right logistics solutions can make all the difference in building a successful supply chain. At Interlake Mecalux, we support companies around the world with cutting-edge storage systems, automation, and specialized software. Feel free to explore more success stories and contact us — we’ll design a customized solution for your business.

What is Supply Chain Logistics? Meaning, Importance, and Key Differences with Logistics Management
Discover how supply chain logistics manages the flow of goods, information, and resources to optimize costs, enhance customer satisfaction, and adapt to challenges, supporting business success and offering students valuable career opportunities in the field

15/11/2024
News
What is Supply Chain Logistics? Meaning and Importance
Supply chain logistics refers to the intricate processes involved in managing the flow of goods, information, and resources from suppliers to end consumers. This field encompasses multiple essential activities like sourcing, production, inventory management, and distribution, ensuring a smooth transfer of products and data across the entire supply chain.
Definition of Supply Chain Logistics
Supply chain logistics can be defined as the orchestration of processes that move products, information, and finances from suppliers to consumers. This broad discipline includes procurement, manufacturing, inventory management, and distribution, all working in harmony to facilitate efficient product flow from raw materials to final delivery. For businesses, supply chain logistics is a crucial backbone, supporting product availability, timely delivery, and cost-efficiency.
For students interested in mastering this field, the Specialized Master in Supply Chain Management and Logistics at Rome Business School offers in-depth knowledge and practical experience to build a successful career.
Importance of Supply Chain Logistics in Business
The significance of supply chain logistics in business cannot be overstated. Effective supply chain logistics enables:
 Reduced Operational Costs: Optimizing routes, storage, and distribution channels allows companies to lower expenses, which can lead to more competitive pricing.
 Improved Customer Satisfaction: Efficient logistics ensure customers receive products on time and in excellent condition, fostering brand loyalty.
 Enhanced Adaptability: Flexible supply chain strategies allow companies to respond quickly to demand changes or disruptions, maintaining steady operations even in fluctuating markets.
For business students, understanding the role of supply chain logistics offers insight into how it influences business success, from profitability and brand reputation to customer loyalty.
Difference Between Logistics and Supply Chain Management
While often used interchangeably, “logistics” and “supply chain management” (SCM) represent different aspects of business operations:
 Scope: Logistics focuses on the storage, movement, and distribution of goods, covering areas like warehousing and order fulfillment. SCM is broader, encompassing logistics and also including procurement, production, and coordination with suppliers and stakeholders.
 Purpose: Logistics aims to deliver goods swiftly and affordably, while SCM seeks efficiency across production stages to ensure that all components—raw materials, manufacturing, logistics, and distribution—work seamlessly.
 Interconnectivity: Logistics involves moving and storing goods, while SCM integrates logistics with other functions (marketing, finance, production) to support the company’s broader objectives.
Understanding these distinctions helps students identify career paths within the field, as logistics and supply chain roles often require unique skill sets.
What is Supply Chain Management and Why is it Important?
Supply chain management (SCM) is the comprehensive oversight of the production flow, from sourcing raw materials to delivering finished products. SCM includes planning, executing, and controlling every aspect of a product’s journey.
The importance of SCM includes:
 Strategic Efficiency: SCM streamlines production by aligning every stage with business goals, enhancing productivity.
 Risk Mitigation: Effective SCM enables quick adjustments to supply and demand fluctuations, minimizing disruption impacts.
 Competitive Advantage: With efficient SCM, companies can respond to market needs faster and at a lower cost, gaining an edge over competitors.
For students, understanding logistics and SCM highlights how these functions support business resilience and growth.
Understanding 3PL and 4PL in the Supply Chain
Recently, companies have increasingly relied on third-party (3PL) and fourth-party logistics (4PL) providers to enhance supply chain operations.
What is 3PL (Third-Party Logistics)?
Third-party logistics (3PL) refers to outsourcing logistics services, such as warehousing, transportation, and distribution, to external providers. Examples include well-known companies like UPS Supply Chain Solutions, which offer specialized resources.
Key benefits of 3PL:
 Scalability: 3PL providers adjust services to meet seasonal or growth needs, enabling easy expansion.
 Cost Savings: Outsourcing logistics often reduces labor, transportation, and warehousing expenses.
 Improved Focus: Companies can concentrate on core activities like product development while 3PL providers handle logistics.
What is 4PL (Fourth-Party Logistics)?
Fourth-party logistics (4PL) goes beyond 3PL by managing the entire supply chain, coordinating suppliers, carriers, and logistics providers on behalf of clients. This comprehensive approach provides:
 Centralized Management: 4PL providers oversee all supply chain aspects, creating a streamlined operation.
 Enhanced Data Insights: Many 4PL providers leverage technology and data analytics for optimized supply chain performance.
 Full Integration: By managing all functions, 4PL providers ensure an integrated, responsive supply chain.
Learning about 3PL and 4PL logistics provides students with insight into how companies can enhance efficiency and responsiveness through strategic outsourcing.
Supply chain logistics and SCM are foundational elements of modern business, supporting processes from raw material procurement to last-mile delivery. Exploring these functions in detail helps students discover which area aligns best with their career aspirations. The field offers numerous opportunities, from logistics roles focused on efficiency to SCM roles that drive strategic alignment and competitive advantage.
For those interested, Rome Business School’s Specialized Master in Supply Chain Management and Logistics provides an excellent foundation for a career in this evolving field.

What is a supply chain?
Explore what a supply chain is and the stages of a supply chain
What is a supply chain?
In its simplest form, a supply chain is the activities required to deliver goods or services to the consumer.
A supply chain focuses on the core activities within your organisation to convert raw materials or component parts into finished products or services. You must look upstream to your suppliers and their supply of raw materials or components and downstream from your organisation's activities through to delivery of the goods of services to the end consumer.
If we look at a traditional manufacturing environment, the activity of interfacing with suppliers is generally supported by ‘procurement.’ The materials will then pass-through goods in warehouses (if products) through to the manufacturing site and onto the finished goods warehouse. This activity is the core activity of ‘operations management.’ Throughout the supply chain, logistics play an integral role in the movement of inbound materials and outbound goods to ensure the finished product flows downstream to the consumer.
A supply chain can take on the form of a product-based supply chain or that of a service. When thinking about a service, services will come together to offer an overall customer service, as opposed to a finished product. An example of this would be the shipping of customers goods. The staff, supply of vessel and fuel are all required to provide the shipping service to the consumer.
When your supply chain is connected with that of your suppliers and consumers, you can then start to build a supply chain network. You can then go onto understand the flow of both materials and information in a much more complex way.
What are the stages within a supply chain?
There are many stages and activities with a supply chain. Porters Value Chain sets out the activities that add value and are split into primary and support activities. Porters Value Chain helps organisations to understand and examine all activities and how they’re connected.
What is supply chain management?
Supply chain management takes a broader view of influences that will impact your supply chain. When discussing supply chain management, you’ll then start to understand the strategic decisions that influence supply chain activity.
Once the strategy has been set for the organisation, any decisions then made within the supply chain must ensure that they deliver against the corporate strategy, this is supply chain management in operation.
What is the main goal of supply chain management?
The goal of supply chain management is to look holistically at the entire supply chain from supplier through to the consumer. It’s then important to review three core areas of people, process, and systems to maximise value from all activities.
Why is supply chain management important?
Effective supply chain management improves the financial position of an organisation by delivering value which is linked to the organisation’s corporate strategy. Supply chain management plays a significant role in customer satisfaction through the delivery of products and services. Good supply chain management is critical at reducing operating costs from procurement activities, through operations and logistics functions and throughout the whole supply chain. The scale of profitability for large organisations is relative to the management of an organisation’s supply chain.
Extended supply chain
Extended supply chains cover every aspect of the process, from identifying the need to the end user consuming the product or service. They promote joined-up thinking and collaboration between all the organisations involved. The purpose of the extended supply chain and its management is to ensure that all organisations meet the same standards, have the same end goal and work to achieve ethical and sustainable processes.

What Is Supply Chain Management?
Supply chain management (SCM) is the oversight of all the aspects of your supply chain. Proper management is vital for optimization, and clear processes will help lead your business to high-quality products. Learn what supply chain management is, strategies to improve it, the challenges that come with it, and what software you can use to streamline the process.
What Is Supply Chain Management?
Supply Chain Management Guide
Supply Chain Management Defined
Supply chain management is the overall process of sourcing, producing, and delivering goods. It's a foundational business function, playing a critical role in controlling costs, supporting high-quality service, and ensuring operational agility for businesses of all types and sizes. The scope of supply chain management spans the entire product journey, from raw material to delivery of a final product.
Core Components of SCM
An effective supply chain process requires interconnected functions that help logistics organizations and businesses guide materials and goods from initial planning and ordering phases to delivery—and sometimes back through a reversal process. Every component plays an essential role in optimizing operations and supporting positive business outcomes. 
Planning
Planning is the foundation of supply chain strategy, as it involves forecasting demand and aligning inventory and resources to meet business and customer needs. Strong planning helps minimize costs while supporting a roadmap for optimized procurement, production, and fulfillment.
For example, a global retailer may use demand forecasting software to understand potential seasonal surges. It can align distribution resources, including staff and inventory, to meet expected holiday rushes.
Sourcing
Sourcing activities focus on identifying, evaluating, and managing suppliers, which include vendors and partners providing raw materials, products, and services. Tasks related to sourcing include negotiating contracts and ensuring continuity and quality of supply.
An example of sourcing is a manufacturer partnering with multiple suppliers to diversify raw material sources, reducing vulnerability in its supply chain and risks of disruptions.
Manufacturing
Manufacturing is the process of transforming raw materials and goods into finished products. The efficiency of manufacturing contributes to the cost, output, and lead times within the supply chain. As part of the manufacturing component, businesses must create production schedules, effectively manage equipment, and ensure compliance with regulatory standards.
An example of manufacturing in supply chain management would be an automotive company implementing lean techniques to reduce waste and accelerate delivery times without negatively impacting product quality.
Delivery and Logistics
The delivery and logistics component of supply chain management ensures goods are transported from source locations and delivered to the right place at the right time. Delivery must support manufacturing and customer receipt of ordered products. Delivery and logistics include shipping, fleet management, and customer support.
For example, a clothing brand may use transportation management systems to optimize delivery routes to retail locations. Better route performance reduces fuel costs and helps support on-time deliveries across large regions.
Returns Management
The supply chain doesn't only move in a single direction. Returns management supports the flow of product back into or up the supply chain. While this process is important for customer returns and handling damaged goods, it's also required for sustainability initiatives, such as recycling and refurbishment.
For example, a technology manufacturer might have processes for buying back used equipment, refurbishing it, and reselling it. Or, a home goods retailer may need a way to accept returns for online orders when customers aren't satisfied with products.   

Strategies in SCM
Supply chain strategies define how businesses approach all the components discussed above, and the right strategies can help align operations with demand and support resilience in challenging markets. Let's look at four common SCM strategies with examples of each.
Lean Supply Chain Management
Lean approaches focus on removing waste from processes. Waste includes:
For example, a company may adopt a lean approach to inventory, ordering only what's needed immediately to minimize the cost of warehousing. However, this approach requires strong demand forecasting to avoid stockouts and processing delays.
Agile Supply Chain Management
Agile approaches support businesses in adapting quickly to changing market conditions, including supply and demand. Key pillars of this approach include flexibility, speed, and responsiveness, which make access to accurate real-time data a must. Logistics teams must also engage in effective cross-functional collaboration to support agile SCM.
For example, if a fashion retailer notices changing trends in the middle or late in the season, it might change suppliers to meet evolving customer expectations.
Just-In-Time (JIT)
Just-in-time approaches involve producing or receiving inventory or goods only as required. JIT, which is often a component of lean and agile approaches, helps cut down on inventory holding costs. However, it requires precise planning, dependable supplier relationships, and a mature supply chain.
An example of just-in-time is seen in print-on-demand (POD) book processes. Independent authors and small press publishers use these processes to substantially reduce publishing costs and make their books available to a wider audience, as copies are printed as ordered.
Total Quality Management (TQM)
Total Quality Management focuses on supporting quality in every part of a supply chain through continuous improvement and data-driven decision-making. TQM seeks to create in-process quality assurance and accountability at all levels of the supply chain.
For example, a pharmaceutical company might leverage TQM to ensure strict controls at every stage of the drug manufacturing and delivery process.
Technology and Innovation in SCM
Modern supply chains run on advanced technology, which helps keep pace with global demand, pivot in response to disruptions, and meet customer expectations. The right technology supports visibility into the entire supply chain and precision across every stage.
Digital transformation has enabled real-time insights and seamless data sharing across supply chains. AI and machine learning help organizations forecast demand based on millions of data points and automate decision-making at a scale that supports global logistics.
The Internet of Things (IoT) offers a way to track assets via scanning, GPS tracking, and other technologies. It also paves the way for more detailed environmental monitoring. All this results in greater control over shipments and inventory throughout the supply chain.
Blockchain offers added transparency and security. It creates immutable records that document transactions throughout the supply chain, supporting more confident customs processes and peace of mind for buyers and sellers alike.
Supply Chain Software
Software plays a critical role in modern supply chain execution. From demand planning to final-mile delivery, businesses need the right digital tools to respond quickly, optimize performance, and scale effectively.
Types of Supply Chain Software
Software works throughout the supply chain and assists with smarter decision-making and continuous improvement. Supply chain planning software supports the strategic aspects of supply chain management, including inventory planning and demand forecasting. These tools help businesses align supply with expected demand, support agile responses, and minimize inventory waste. Examples of supply chain planning software include:
Supply chain execution software helps companies manage the flow of resources, including physical goods, labor, and data. Examples of these tools include:

Integration Capabilities
Many supply chain software solutions are cloud-native, which supports seamless integration. Planning and executing may be able to be done on the same platform, or solutions can be integrated to support a unified approach for holistic logistics. Integration reduces silos between departments and provides a more coordinated supply chain.
Keep in mind, your software shouldn’t just be able to integrate with your systems. If you’re using multiple software solutions, they need unification — the ability to communicate, share data, and work seamlessly with each other.
Challenges and Risk Management
Even the most optimized supply chains can face challenges that increase costs or negatively impact customer experience. Challenges that affect production or delivery include natural disasters, geopolitical situations, and supplier failures.
It's impossible to always know when disruptions might occur, so proactive risk management strategies are critical. Planning, supplier diversification, and real-time monitoring can help reduce risk vulnerability or ensure rapid resolution when issues arise. Scenario modeling helps businesses understand the impact of various potential events so they can create flexible sourcing strategies to improve logistics resilience.
Global operations must also adhere to evolving regulations related to safety, trade, and the environment. Because noncompliance can result in delays and additional costs, often in the form of fines or harm to business reputation, it's crucial to consider these factors when managing supply chains.  

Sustainability and Ethical Considerations
The importance of supply chain management extends beyond efficiency. Minimizing environmental impact through lower emissions, reduced waste, and responsible sourcing is an essential component of most modern SCM processes. Sustainable practices help companies meet regulations across the globe and may be necessary to meet ever-changing customer preferences.
Ethical supply chains are also important—they emphasize fair labor, long-term economic stability, and transparency. Integrating these values into supply chain management helps strengthen an organization and build trust with customers and global supply partners.
The Future of SCM
AI-driven automation, predictive analytics, and demand for better end-to-end visibility will continue to shape supply chain management in the near future. These technologies will only get more adept at supporting real-time access to logistics information.
Globalization continues to expand supplier networks, which is a good thing for SCM. However, it also introduces risks and complexities that smaller or more regional businesses might not have dealt with before. Building a resilient network via flexible sourcing, real-time data, and integrated planning becomes more essential every day for companies that want to compete in modern markets.
Put Supply Chain Management Into Action
Effective supply chain management drives efficiency and agility to support long-term success. With increasing globalization and more complex networks for businesses of all types, advanced tools are required to stay competitive.
Manhattan’s best-in-class supply chain management solutions include supply chain execution, planning, and order management software. These solutions all integrate seamlessly to facilitate more efficient communication and effective planning.

Meanwhile, Manhattan Active Warehouse Management offers a future-proof cloud-native solution that integrates with your entire network to facilitate real-time decision making.

Optimize Every Stage of Transportation

Supply Chain Planning
Enjoy unified forecasting, replenishment, and allocation systems in a powerful cloud-native platform. Manhattan Active Supply Chain Planning offers a microservice API for easy integration with other tools and delivers AI-powered demand forecasting, real-time inventory management, and dynamic replenishment systems.

FAQs
What Is Supply Chain Management?
Supply chain management refers to the oversight and coordination of people, technology, and data required to transport goods. Effective supply chain management enhances efficiency, reduces costs, and improves service by optimizing the processes across the supply chain.
What Is Supply Chain Management Software?
Supply chain management software refers to technology that supports supply chain management by improving visibility, automation, and efficiency across the supply chain. Examples of supply chain management software include solutions to help manage inventory, forecast demand, and optimize agile response to disruptions.
What Are the 5 Main Functions of Supply Chain Management?
The core functions of supply chain management include:
How Can Supply Chain Software Help Improve Supply Chain Operations?
Supply chain software enhances operations by:
In short, the right software tools help companies plan better, fulfill orders faster, and respond better to disruptions and other changes.

The Importance of Supply Chain Management in Logistics
By Dr. Robert Gordon, CPC  |  11/14/2023
importance of supply chain management in logisticsMany people ask me what the most essential aspect of a good supply chain is, and in my mind, the most crucial factor is flexibility. A supply chain can do many things, but to be effective, a supply chain must be flexible.
importance of supply chain management in logistics
Most of the time, items in a supply chain are in motion, so it's imperative that a supply chain have flexible logistics. Flexible logistics allow for the expediting or redirection of material, and being able to push or pull materials to and from different locations is very powerful.
For instance, if a critical part is unavailable but also required to repair equipment, a manufacturing line might go down. The longer the production line is down, the more time will be lost and production will drop.
Without a finished product available for sale, sales will decrease. As sales decrease, customers might seek alternatives and buy from another business. Forcing customers to seek alternatives allows competitors to erode a company's market share.
This illustration of why flexibility is so essential to an organization’s logistics is just one example of why flexible logistics are necessary. Flexible logistics can reduce costs by helping avoid mistakes and make needed changes when issues arise. Keeping customer requirements in focus and shipping products on schedule, can help make the customer experience a positive one.
Related: The Business World and the Future Post-COVID-19 Environment
What Is Logistics Management?
Logistics management involves the flow of goods and services from the point of origin to the point of consumption. It encompasses various activities, including:
Logistics managers control where shipments are routed, and coordinators keep end users updated about deliveries. A qualified logistician considers factors such as shipping cost, time, and environmental impact, among other aspects of the chosen mode of transport.
Related: How to Create and Improve Cybersecurity for Small Businesses
The Importance of Transportation
Transportation is one of the most crucial aspects of the logistics industry. Transportation involves selecting the most appropriate mode of transport for goods or materials – such as road, rail, sea, or air – and planning the most efficient route to move goods from one location to another.
Transportation has become particularly challenging due to a current personnel shortage impacting trucking, rail, ocean, and air shipments. As a result, both inbound and outbound logistics are affected.
Effective Inventory Management Within Fulfillment Centers
Logistics is all about getting material to the right place at the right time. Logistics management requires precision and should operate like clockwork. Managing inventory is closely related to warehousing and fulfillment centers, and it involves keeping track of the quantities and locations of goods in storage.
Too much inventory in fulfillment centers will increase costs and reduce effectiveness. Too much inventory is "dead" money, as inventory only generates revenue when it is sold. Carrying inventory costs the company money that could be used elsewhere.
Too little inventory will increase the downtime of equipment and can lead to shortages, like the great toilet paper shortage during the COVID-19 pandemic. It is necessary to keep the warehouse management system working efficiently and to use efficient demand planning with the most accurate information possible.
Ensuring Efficiency in Distribution Centers
Logistics management also involves moving materials in and out of distribution centers. Efficient distribution centers prioritize managing storage space to ensure that goods can be quickly located and retrieved when needed, then sent to the end user.
Every organization needs to have a logistics strategy to focus efforts and information in a manner that supports operations. Logistics management is a critical function for any business producing or distributing goods and services to a brick-and-mortar store or to an ecommerce business.
A good first step for a logistics strategy is to map out the organization’s logistics processes. Understanding all the various logistics components and process components can help a company ensure customer satisfaction, while reducing costs and improving efficiency.
Using logistics management software can help an organization better track – and understand how logistics plays into – the movement of all goods. Consider the examples of Walmart® and Amazon®, as much of their growth has been driven by successfully managing all their inbound logistics and outbound logistics. Walmart and Amazon have become important business logistics companies with their efficient order processing and their own branded delivery trucks.
Many other companies have implemented automated systems for customer ordering, warehouse management, and material handling. Organizations have even begun to use autonomous vehicles for shipping services. These systems aim to lower costs and often help by simplifying communication across the supply chain system.
Why Is Supply Chain Management Important in Logistics?
The importance of cost reduction looms over every organization. Effective logistics management helps in reducing the overall costs of a company. It involves:
The optimization of operations can reduce transportation and warehousing costs. Considering the global economy and many company supply chains' global nature, transportation is a significant component of total supply chain costs.
Being cost-effective is always a concern for companies; however, in many cases, efficiency in logistics is more important than reducing direct costs. For example, if the production line needs a lot of bearings to keep things moving, one company might look to get the longest-lasting bearings for that production line.
You might ask, “Why not get cheaper bearings, since the direct costs would be lower?” Although that is true, the cost of the labor to replace the bearings will outweigh the direct costs of the bearings themselves.
Bearings are never in an easy-to-access places and are generally inside specialized equipment, requiring a lot of labor to disassemble and reassemble equipment to replace the worn part. Ultimately, having a more expensive bearing that lasts twice as long and avoids twice as much labor (as well as shipping costs) is better.
The Differences Between Managing Logistics and Supply Chains
There are some similarities between supply chain management and logistics, but there are also some key differences. The management of a supply chain is a broader concept in business, and logistics management involves a wide range of activities involved in producing and delivering goods and services.
The management of a supply chain covers the entire production process. It involves coordinating and managing sourcing, procurement, and logistics activities.
Supply chain management also includes managing relationships with suppliers, manufacturers, and distributors, as well as planning and controlling the flow of goods from production to the consumer.
Similarly, logistics management focuses on planning, implementing, and controlling the flow of goods and services. However, it also involves transportation management, warehousing, inventory management, and order fulfillment.
Logistics refers to the movement and storage of goods and services and is an area that supply chain logisticians in the logistics industry should know well. By contrast, managing a supply chain involves a broader range of activities, including the sourcing of raw materials, procurement, production, and distribution.
At the same time, aspects of the logistics industry such as inbound logistics, outbound logistics, and business logistics involves a subset of supply chain management, focused on moving and storing goods and services. These sectors of the supply chain are crucial for the success of a business and are closely related, but they are not the same thing.
Increasing Supply Chain Efficiency Provides a Unique Advantage
Efficient supply chain management can lead to a competitive advantage, underscoring the importance of supply chain management in logistics. An effective supply chain can help reduce costs while improving efficiency and customer satisfaction.
Keeping costs down and customer satisfaction high is the recipe for a successful company and its supply chain, especially in a crowded market. Every organization needs to evaluate the management of its supply chain and improve any of its poor logistics processes to better understand the efficiencies the company can achieve. In turn, that can lead to an improved customer experience and greater customer satisfaction.

5 Key Components And Parts Of A Supply Chain : Explained In 2026

.png)

5 Key Components And Parts Of A Supply Chain : Explained In 2026
Explore the key components of a modern supply chain and how each stage improves efficiency, cost savings, and customer value.

Understanding the different components of a modern supply chain is essential for any business that wants to stay efficient, reduce costs, and meet customer expectations. When these components work together effectively, companies can respond faster to demand, manage risks better, and improve overall performance. Without clarity on how these critical components function and connect, businesses risk delays, overspending, and missed opportunities.
‍
This blog is for finance leaders, procurement teams, and operations managers who want to build a smarter, more resilient supply chain. It will help you identify the most important areas to focus on, and how each stage can be improved with the right strategies and tools.
‍
‍
What is Supply Chain?
A supply chain is the entire network involved in producing and delivering a product or service - from sourcing raw materials to manufacturing, warehousing, transportation, and final delivery to the customer. It ensures goods flow efficiently from start to finish.
‍
Supply Chain Stages
A modern supply chain includes interconnected stages that ensure the supply chain process from planning to final delivery.
‍
1. Demand Planning and Forecasting
Demand demand management aligns operations with market needs, align inventory, and prepare operations for changing market conditions.
‍
‍What Can Go Wrong: Businesses may overproduce or understock, leading to waste, lost sales, and unhappy customers.
‍
‍What Can Go Right: Companies meet demand accurately, reduce holding costs, and streamline operations across the components of the supply chain.
‍
2. Procurement and Supplier Management
This stage ensures timely sourcing and cost-effective supplier partnerships to maintain steady material flow.
‍
‍What Can Go Wrong: Delayed supplies, poor quality inputs, and higher costs disrupt production schedule and resource timelines.
‍
‍What Can Go Right: Companies gain control over spend, reduce supplier risk, and maintain consistent material flow.
‍
‍
‍
3. Production Planning and Scheduling
Coordinates materials, labor, and equipment to meet production targets with minimal delays or resource waste.
‍
What Can Go Wrong: Idle equipment or overloaded production lines lead to delays, rework, and increased expenses.
‍
What Can Go Right: Operations run smoothly with balanced workloads, efficient output, and minimal disruption.
‍
4. Inventory and Warehouse Management
Ensures stock accuracy, optimal storage, and fast access for better order fulfillment.
‍
What Can Go Wrong: Overstocking ties up cash; stockouts cause missed orders and emergency shipments.
‍
What Can Go Right: Inventory stays lean and accurate, reducing carrying costs and improving order response times.
‍
‍
5. Transportation and Logistics
Manages how goods move through the supply chain to reach destinations on time and within budget.
‍
What Can Go Wrong: Late deliveries, high freight costs, and routing issues affect service quality and margins.
‍
What Can Go Right: Goods arrive on time, costs stay controlled, and customer expectations are consistently met.
‍
6. Delivery and Order Fulfillment
Focuses on accurate, fast, and reliable shipping to customers and partners.
‍
What Can Go Wrong: Errors in shipment or delays hurt customer service and satisfaction and loyalty.
‍
‍What Can Go Right: Fast, accurate fulfillment drives trust, repeat business, and competitive advantage.
‍
Procurement as a Core Component
Procurement plays a critical role in keeping the supply chain moving. It ensures the timely availability of goods and services while optimizing costs, managing risks, and maintaining quality. Without efficient procurement, the entire supply chain can face costly disruptions and inefficiencies.
‍
A) How procurement drives the supply chain forward
Procurement is the engine that powers the supply chain. It connects planning to production by sourcing the materials needed to deliver goods and services on time. When done right, it ensures that all other supply chain functions operate smoothly and cost-effectively.
‍
B) Strategic Sourcing and Supplier Selection
Strategic sourcing goes beyond price - it focuses on value, reliability, and long-term fit. Selecting the right suppliers means evaluating performance, capacity, cost, and risk. A proactive sourcing strategy builds resilience, reduces lead times, and strengthens supplier relationships that are vital to long-term success.
‍
C) Procurement's Role in Cost Control and Quality Assurance
Procurement directly impacts both spend and product quality. By negotiating favorable terms and enforcing quality standards, procurement teams help avoid budget overruns and substandard deliveries. This ensures consistency, customer satisfaction, and overall supply chain efficiency.
‍
Direct vs Indirect Procurement: Differences and Importance
Direct and indirect procurement serve different purposes but are equally important in maintaining business continuity and controlling spend.
‍
| Aspect | Direct Procurement | Indirect Procurement |
 --- 
| Purpose | Acquires goods/services used in production | Acquires goods/services for internal use |
| Impact on Business | Directly affects product delivery and quality | Supports day-to-day operations |
| Examples | Raw materials, components, OEM parts | Office supplies, software, marketing services |
| Procurement Approach | Volume-driven, long-term supplier relationships | Cost-focused, often decentralized |
‍
‍
Logistics, Warehousing & Distribution For Supply Chain Components
This component ensures that goods flow efficiently from suppliers to distribution centers and from warehouses to customers. Coordinated logistics and well-managed distribution channels are essential for meeting delivery timelines, minimizing costs, and maintaining service quality across the supply chain.
‍
A) Logistics as the Backbone of Timely Delivery
Logistics connects every stage of the supply chain through transportation, routing, and scheduling. It ensures that raw materials, components, and finished products reach the right place at the right time. Without efficient logistics, delays, disruptions, and cost overruns are unavoidable.
‍
B) Warehousing Strategies and Inventory Control
Modern warehousing goes beyond storage - it enables fast picking, packing, and shipping.
Smart inventory control within warehouses helps inventory optimization, ensures stock is available, improves order accuracy, and reduces waste. Techniques like zone picking, cross-docking, and real-time tracking boost overall efficiency.
‍
C) Distribution Channels and Last-Mile Delivery
Distribution bridges the gap between warehouse and end customer. It includes retail, wholesale, e-commerce, and direct-to-consumer networks. Last-mile delivery is often the most expensive and complex step, yet it defines the customer experience. Optimizing this stage is crucial for satisfaction and loyalty.
‍
D) The Impact on Efficiency and Costs
Transportation decisions - like carrier selection, route planning, and freight mode - have a major impact on both cost and speed. Delays, underutilized shipments, or poor route management can increase expenses.
Strategic transport planning ensures timely delivery and keeps costs under control.
‍
E) Reverse Logistics and Returns Management
Handling product returns, repairs, and recycling is essential for customer satisfaction and sustainability.
A strong reverse logistics process reduces waste, recovers value, and enhances post-purchase experience.
‍
F) Technology in Logistics Operations
Tools like GPS tracking, route optimization software, and warehouse automation improve visibility and speed. These technologies help reduce manual errors, lower operational costs, and increase delivery accuracy.
‍
Supplier Relationships For Supply Chain Components
Strong supplier relationships are the foundation of a dependable, cost-effective, and agile supply chain. When companies collaborate closely with suppliers, they gain better visibility into timelines, pricing, and inventory planning. This collaboration reduces surprises and creates opportunities for joint problem-solving and innovation.
‍
A) Why Collaboration with Suppliers Is Critical
Suppliers aren’t just vendors - they’re strategic partners. Open communication and alignment with suppliers help ensure consistency in quality, delivery, and compliance. A collaborative relationship improves planning accuracy and supports long-term business goals for both parties.
‍
B) Managing Supplier Risk and Performance
Supplier performance directly affects operational continuity. Businesses need clear criteria to evaluate quality, delivery timelines, pricing consistency, and responsiveness. Regular performance reviews and risk assessments help prevent issues like delays, cost overruns, or non-compliance with regulations.
‍
C) Building Resilient Supplier Networks
A resilient supplier network can absorb shocks - such as price volatility, material shortages, or geopolitical disruptions. Companies should diversify their supplier base, assess financial health, and implement contingency plans to reduce over-dependence on single sources.
‍
D) The Role of Long-Term Contracts and Partnerships
Long-term contracts establish stability and foster trust. They allow companies to negotiate better pricing, secure capacity, and lock in quality standards. Strategic partnerships with key suppliers also encourage innovation and shared investment in process improvements.
‍
‍
The Role of Technology in Supply Chains
Technology is no longer optional in today’s supply chains - it’s essential. Digital tools provide real-time visibility, predictive insights, and greater control over processes. From procurement to delivery, technology  helps teams make faster, data-driven decisions that reduce costs and increase agility.
‍
1. How Digital Tools Improve Visibility and Control
Cloud platforms and dashboards enable centralized data across procurement, inventory, and logistics. This transparency helps identify bottlenecks, track performance, and proactively manage supply chain risks in real time.
‍
2. AI, ML, and IoT Applications in Modern Supply Chains
Artificial intelligence and machine learning help predict demand, optimize routes, and automate decision-making. IoT sensors offer real-time tracking of goods, equipment health, and environmental conditions - enabling smarter logistics and inventory management.
‍
3. ERP and SCM Software in Integration and Automation
Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) and Supply Chain Management (SCM) tools break down data silos by connecting teams across sourcing, finance, warehousing, and shipping. They automate routine tasks and enable end-to-end process efficiency.
‍
‍
4. Blockchain for Traceability and Trust
Blockchain technology offers a secure, tamper-proof way to record every transaction and movement in the supply chain. This improves traceability, prevents fraud, and supports compliance - especially in industries with strict regulatory requirements.
‍
Supply Chain KPIs
Effective supply chain management requires the right performance metrics, businesses need to track the right performance metrics. Supply chain KPIs (Key Performance Indicators) offer visibility into efficiency, cost, service levels, and overall operational health. These metrics help identify gaps, make informed decisions, and measure progress toward strategic goals.
‍
1. Most Critical Metrics to Track Supply Chain Performance
Not all KPIs are created equal. The most valuable ones directly impact customer satisfaction, managing operating costs, and delivery speed. These include cycle time, order accuracy, inventory turnover, supplier lead time, and cost-to-serve. Tracking these metrics enables teams to pinpoint inefficiencies and focus improvement efforts where it matters most.
‍
2. Cycle Time, Fill Rate, and Inventory Turnover
Cycle time refers to how long it takes from order placement to fulfillment - a lower number indicates greater efficiency. Fill rate measures how well customer demand is met without delays or backorders, while inventory turnover reflects how often stock is sold and replaced. High turnover means better cash flow and less capital tied up in inventory.
‍
3. Supplier Performance and Cost-to-Serve
Supplier KPIs help evaluate reliability, quality, and responsiveness. Metrics such as on-time delivery rate, defect rates, and communication speed are vital to supplier evaluation. Cost-to-serve, meanwhile, provides a full picture of how much it costs to deliver products to specific customers or markets, including shipping, handling, and service costs.
‍
4. Using KPIs for Continuous Improvement and Agility
KPIs are not just for reporting - they are tools for ongoing improvement. By regularly reviewing performance data, supply chain leaders can make agile adjustments, reduce waste, and better align with business goals. Over time, this leads to a leaner, more responsive, and customer-focused supply chain.
‍
How Spendflo Optimizes Your Supply Chain Operations
Spendflo helps companies gain complete visibility into their SaaS procurement, supplier contracts, and renewals - key areas that directly support supply chain performance. By centralizing vendor data, automating approval workflows, and benchmarking costs, Spendflo streamlines sourcing and supplier management.
‍
This leads to faster decisions, better vendor relationships, and lower spend. Whether you're managing indirect procurement tools or SaaS platforms that support logistics, Spendflo brings clarity, control, and cost savings.
‍
Our platform helps finance, procurement, and operations teams align on spend priorities while building more resilient and efficient supply chain processes from end to end. Start building stronger supplier relationships and get complete control over your contracts!
‍
Book a free demo with Spendflo today.
‍
‍
See How Spendflo Helps Companies Save Thousands
BetterPlace achieved 4x ROI and streamlined vendor governance using Spendflo - centralizing intake, standardizing processes, and improving compliance across teams.
BetterPlace Banner
‍
Frequently Asked Questions on Supply Chain Components
What Are the Key Components of a Supply Chain?
The key components include demand planning, procurement, production, inventory management, logistics, and delivery. Each stage ensures smooth and timely movement of goods.
‍
How Does Procurement Fit into the Supply Chain?
Procurement connects planning to production by sourcing materials and services. It manages costs, ensures supply availability, and maintains supplier relationships.
‍
What Technologies Improve Supply Chain Performance?
Technologies like ERP systems, AI/ML tools, IoT devices, and blockchain enhance visibility, automation, forecasting, and traceability across the supply chain.
‍
Why Are Supplier Relationships Important in Supply Chain Management?
Strong supplier relationships reduce risk, improve quality and delivery consistency, and enable collaboration for long-term innovation and cost efficiency.
‍
Which KPIs Are Most Useful in Measuring Supply Chain Success?
Useful KPIs include cycle time, fill rate, inventory turnover, supplier on-time delivery, and cost-to-serve.  These metrics help track efficiency and service quality.

What Are the Five Components of Supply Chain Management?

Written by Rebecca Turley
From Raw Material to Finished Product: The Five Components of Supply Chain Management
components in supply chain management
What Are the Five Components of Supply Chain Management? Given the sheer size of today’s global enterprises, it’s easy to envision supply chain management as a highly complex process with tentacles reaching out in every direction to every corner of the globe. But despite all its moving parts, even the most intricate global supply chains can be broken down into five major components – planning and analysis, sourcing, manufacturing, distribution… and in the case of ecommerce giants like Amazon, there’s also returns.
Supply chain management comprises a product’s entire lifecycle. It encompasses all the people, processes, activities, and resources that take a product from a raw material to a finished consumer good. Supply chains are built, maintained, and constantly fine-tuned to minimize risk, streamline the links that connect the process, and gain a competitive advantage in the marketplace.
Featured Programs:
Sponsored School(s)
Liberty University Logo 
Liberty University
Featured Program: MBA: Supply Chain Management & Logistics; DBA: Supply Chain Management & Logistics; MS: Healthcare Administration: Supply Chain Management & Logistics; PHD: Business Administration: Supply Chain Management & Logistics
Request Info

Featured Program: MBA with concentration in Operations and Supply Chain Management
Request Info

Arizona State University - Online
Featured Program: Supply Chain Management, BS
Request Info
Ohio University Logo 
Ohio University
Featured Program: Master of Business Administration - Operations and Supply Chain Management Concentration; Professional Master of Business Administration - Operations and Supply Chain Management Concentration; Operations and Supply Chain Management Certificate
Request Info
Companies of every scope and size call on the talents of supply chain managers and leaders to oversee the supply chain management process and ensure that it’s efficient, cost-effective, and resilient. And in today’s global landscape, where supply chain activities occur in all corners of the world, their role has never been more important.
Supply chains can be short, consisting of just a few links that take a collection of domestically sourced raw materials and components through manufacturing to become a market-ready product … Or it can consist of a highly complex network of suppliers and partners, touching hundreds of points around the globe over the course of many months before finally being ready to ship to wholesalers, placed on retail store shelves, or direct to consumers. But in all cases, it can be broken down into these five components:
1. Planning and Analysis
planning and analysis
You can’t put a solid plan into motion without proper planning. Supply chain management begins with crafting a thoughtful strategy that’s built around identifying and studying a company’s current and future needs.
A good plan consists of strategies aimed at:
 Negotiating supply and demand
 Assembling and organizing the resources, people, and materials required to meet a company’s needs
 Minimizing operating costs
 Streamlining operations
There are several steps within the planning and analysis component of supply chain management:
 Demand Planning: The planning process considers supply and demand, with most companies kickstarting the process with demand planning – determining supply and demand based on historical data, projected sales, market conditions, and other factors. The information gleaned through demand planning is passed down to all teams within the supply chain, who then coordinate their efforts to achieve an organizational goal.   
Demand planning serves as the foundation off which all other supply chain processes operate. Much of today’s demand planning is accomplished using advanced technologies like AI and machine learning that apply real-time data, historical sales, and market conditions to make accurate predictions.
 Supply Planning: Once supply and demand are determined, supply chain management professionals must begin creating the strategy that will put the plan into motion.   
Just a small sampling of the issues considered during the supply planning stage include: What raw materials are needed? Where will the materials be sourced? How and where will manufacturing take place? What equipment is needed to produce the product? Who will provide warehousing and distributing activities, and how will the product make its way to the supplier, retailer, or consumer?
 Production Planning: What does the production of the product look like? How will the company produce what is needed without creating an inventory surplus?
 Sales and Operation Planning: During the sales and operations planning stage, the sales and marketing teams come together to ensure their plans match operational objectives.
2. Sourcing (Procurement)

Sourcing is often seen as one of the most critical components of the supply chain management process, as it is where some of the most significant cost savings can be achieved. Sourcing encompasses the entire procurement process, which includes selecting suppliers, ordering, receiving, and managing inventory, and then authorizing supplier payments.
For many companies, the sourcing process is highly complex, with large organizations sometimes working with thousands of suppliers. For example, Starbucks works with some 300,000 coffee growers around the world to source its coffee beans.
Supply chain management professionals identify suppliers that meet a company’s needs and negotiate contracts to receive the necessary goods. They also focus much of their efforts on nurturing supplier relationships and maintaining a diversified network of suppliers to mitigate risks and quickly remediate issues that could affect supply shortages.
For example, a variety of issues in global trade can disrupt the delicate supply/demand balance. Geo-political pressures, tariffs, regulatory barriers, and infrastructure bottlenecks are just some of the challenges associated with importing and exporting across international borders.
Even regional and local issues can create major supply chain backups. For example, a major snowstorm along the eastern seaboard can thwart the movement of goods and severely disrupt a manufacturer’s supply chain. Therefore, manufacturers are always best served by maintaining a network of suppliers in different geographic regions to head issues off at the pass. Supply chain management professionals must be able to pivot their sourcing plans on a moment’s notice to prevent supply chain disruptions.
3. Manufacturing and Production (Operations)
manufacturing and production
The manufacturing and production component of supply chain management is where the magic happens, when planning and sourcing efforts come to fruition to allow the company to transform raw materials and components into the finished product.
During this central stage of the supply chain process, it’s the responsibility of supply chain management professionals to oversee all activities associated with the in-house production of goods, including product testing, production output, packaging, inventory optimization, and quality assurance. Supply chain managers are also on the lookout for any issues and for opportunities to fine-tune the sourcing or manufacturing process.
4. Distribution (Transportation, Warehousing, & Logistics)
distribution
The distribution stage of the supply chain management process encompasses the activities associated with getting the finished product to consumers.
At this stage, supply chain management professionals (1) determine the logistics of how the product will move efficiently to the next step in the supply chain; (2) implement formal distribution plans; and (3) work with distribution partners to move the finished product through one or more distribution channels:
 Direct sales – Manufacturers sell directly to the consumer.
 Wholesale – Wholesalers purchase products in bulk from manufacturers to resell to retailers.
 Brokerage – Manufacturers use brokers to act as middlemen to negotiate sales with retailers.
 Dual distribution – Some manufacturers and vendors employ different distribution channels for getting their products into customers' hands. This could mean B2B sales, selling to wholesalers, and directly to consumers.
The distribution component of supply chain management explores how inventory will make its way into the hands of wholesalers, distributors, and retailers.
Inventory and warehousing management software and leading-edge technologies allow supply chain managers to better plan the distribution process and put plans in motion that make it more resilient and reliable.
5. Returning (Reverse Logistics)

Also often referred to as reverse logistics, the returning component of supply chain management is the process of receiving returned products and the protocol for assigning refunds. Returns may be associated with product recalls, damaged or defective products, or simply consumer dissatisfaction.
This stage has the potential to create a ripple effect throughout the entire supply chain, particularly when dealing with products that don’t meet quality or safety standards. Strong supply chain management leaders during this stage work to identify product issues and coordinate the actions necessary to remediate them.

metadata.title: What Are the Five Components of Supply Chain Management?

documents.page_content:
8 Vital Components of Successful Supply Chain Management
Written by
Updated December 19, 2025
Any business that sells products to a consumer, whether it’s an individual or another business, has to develop a process for manufacturing, storing and distributing its products. Supply chain management can manifest in different ways from one business to another, depending on the industry, and results in a more effective and cost-efficient process. If you’re a supply chain professional or you want to become one, learning more about the fundamental components within the supply chain can be beneficial.In this article, we define supply chain and provide a list of supply chain components for you to learn more about.
Your personal AI career coach is on the app
Find and apply to jobs 7x faster with Career Scout, only available on the app
Learn moreSource: Indeed Data, US, based on median, compared to job seekers who use mobile app
What is a supply chain?
A supply chain is a system of steps that are set up between a company and the suppliers and vendors it does business with to make sure its buyers receive the goods and products they purchase. Businesses may develop a supply chain so they have a process in place that helps them be more cost-efficient, able to compete with similar businesses and establish a production process that is quicker and more thorough.Related: 20 Supply Chain Management Interview Questions
8 supply chain components
An effective supply chain ensures that products, goods and materials deliver on time and within budget. Here are the components of supply chain management:
1. Planning
Planning involves thinking through all the facets and details of supply chain management so you're prepared for how the entire process commences. The planning stage is where you ask questions, including:
 Will we manufacture the product ourselves or outsource that part of the process?
 Will we make products to order or keep a stock of our goods?
 Should we use a supplier for our products? If we do, should they be a domestic or an international supplier?
 Will we configure our items to order or engineer them according to customers' specifications?
During planning, you develop supply chain strategies that match your overarching company objectives and strategies, build communication channels that support the supply chain, determine how productive the business needs to be to meet customer and supplier expectations and establish performance measurement guidelines and data gathering methods.Related: 12 Supply Chain Certifications To Advance Your Career
2. Information
Securing a method to gather and organize information is an important part of supply chain management. This component may include completing market trend research and assessing the current supply and demand. Consider integrating data storage and analysis into your supply chain management approach to help with decision-making and avoid mistakes and delays.
3. Suppliers and vendors
The second component of supply chain management is the sourcing of goods and materials. This involves finding suppliers and vendors that you can work with to complete your supply chain process. Consider performing research on potential suppliers and vendors, negotiating your contracts with them and making sure they are able to support your company's business needs. It's also important to make sure that any other company you source from can meet your production demands and manage inventory accordingly.Related: What Is Vendor Management?
4. Inventory build-up
Understanding demand can help you plan for inventory, and having too much inventory can be costly. Comparatively, if you don't have enough inventory to meet demand, you can upset customers or have to spend more money or a premium to increase production in the short term.Build up your inventory based on your demand forecasting so you can have products available for purchase, staying a little ahead of demands to avoid any issues with production and maintenance.
5. Production
Another component of supply chain management is your production activities. This is when you schedule production and test your production plans to make sure they support your product demands and the company's goals. The production stage also includes the packing of products, complying with rules and regulations for your industry, data storage and developing production performance standards.Related: Top 11 Production Skills To Develop
6. Storage
Just as inventory is important, so too is the storage of that inventory. Explore warehouses or smaller offices that can house your inventory based on your current or projected product demands. Consider the size of your storage space, if your products require air conditioning, if you share the space with other companies and any other factors that are important to keep in mind as you source a place for your inventory.
7. Distribution
This is one of the first major components where customers get more of a hands-on experience with your product. Distribution includes transporting goods, materials and products from one location to another, typically from the warehouse or manufacturing plant to a store or the customer's home. Set up the transportation process to meet your needs.For example, determine if you need to acquire or hire an air-conditioned vehicle and how big the vehicle should be based on projected sales. You may also want to check the safety reports and delivery times for the companies you're considering working with.Customer service is also part of the distribution process in which you handle customer inquiries, decide how to distribute your products, choose self-distribution or opt for a company to handle the majority of the process for you, activate a warranty period and process invoices and payments.
8. Product returns
This part of the supply chain stems from the customer or business to which you provide goods or products. It's important to prepare for a situation where your customer wants to return the product they've purchased from you.Because returns are a normal part of business when you provide a good or product to another party, the more you're able to develop a process for returns, the more prepared you are for the inevitability. These are some factors to consider when creating a plan for product returns:
Reasons for return
Here are some reasons a customer may request a return:
 The product is defective.
 Their purchase didn't meet expectations.
 They bought the product for cheaper elsewhere.
 They no longer need the product.
Customer service
Most customers expect a fairly smooth process to return products they no longer want or need, and the same for any exchange process they choose to go through to get a replacement product. This includes the process of communicating with any customer service representatives about their request.Because there are established expectations, it's important to meet or exceed those expectations wherever possible so you can retain your customers and provide the kind of service that results in increased sales, hopefully influencing your customers to share their experiences with others.
Returns planning
Consider how you process returns, including how you accept returns and what instructions you provide to customers, how you manage exchange requests and how you handle products you receive from customers. During the return stage, monitor how often you receive returns and why, manage your inventory of the products you've received from customer returns and provide refunds.
