documents.page_content:
Seattle University
Understanding the Key Elements of Supply Chain Management (SCM)
illustration of global locations in a supply chain
As the labor shortages and pandemic closings of recent years have shown, interruptions in the supply chain can easily cause chaos in the economy. Modern business depends on a resilient global supply chain to function effectively.1
In a manufacturing supply chain, suppliers provide raw materials to manufacturers who produce goods. These finished goods are sent to distributors, who handle logistics and inventory management. Retailers purchase products from distributors to sell to end customers, the final link in the supply chain.
Beginning with the supplier, each link in the chain has to perform its role well to support profitable operations for the business and satisfaction for the end customer.2 Supply chain management professionals can enhance operations for each supply chain member organization. This article will explore how today's supply chain participants work together for smooth business operations.
Suppliers: The Foundation of SCM
Suppliers are key players in the supply chain who provide the raw materials and fabricated components manufacturers need to make products. Whether it’s through high-quality raw materials or defect-free components, suppliers' quality directly influences the final product's quality. When they deliver materials that meet specifications on time, suppliers help manufacturers meet production schedules, maintain consistency in lead times, and achieve customer satisfaction.3
As the first link in the chain, suppliers can significantly contribute to their customers' success in various ways. In addition to supporting cost management by delivering reliably, they can help mitigate risks by providing alternative sources during disruptions. Suppliers can enhance the brand's reputation when they cater to environmental and ethical considerations and practice sustainable sourcing.3
Strong Relationships with Suppliers Support Resilient Supply Chains
Building strong supplier relationships is essential for supply chain success, and you can use several tactics to build a strong supplier network. Begin with the procurement process, choosing reliable suppliers through thorough research and audits. Clear communication about expectations and regular performance reviews are crucial. Treat suppliers as valued partners and invest time developing long-term relationships based on trust and mutual benefits. This collaboration can lead to strategic partnerships creating a competitive advantage for both the manufacturer and the supplier by speeding up the time to market and enhancing product quality.3
However, given the complexities of the global market, manufacturers need to build diversified supplier networks for a reliable and responsive supply base. Building long-term relationships with diverse suppliers can help manufacturers guarantee a steady flow of materials and respond quickly to market changes and disruptions.3
Manufacturers: The Powerhouse
Manufacturers play a central role in the supply chain by converting raw materials into finished goods. Their efficiency and quality influence overall business success, impacting costs, product quality, and customer satisfaction. Finely tuned operations can enhance lead times and achieve cost management goals. Effective management of several interconnected processes is integral to successful manufacturing operations.4
Demand Forecasting
Accurate demand forecasting helps manufacturers determine how much raw material to procure and when to start production. This ensures optimal resource allocation and reduces the risk of overproduction or stockouts.4
Receiving
Receiving involves inspecting delivered materials to ensure they meet quality standards and match purchase orders, safeguarding subsequent production phases from substandard inputs.4
Inventory Management
Efficient inventory management tracks inventory through the production process from raw materials to finished goods, ensuring materials are available when needed without overstocking or running short and holding up production.4 Just-in-Time (JIT) is a lean manufacturing approach that focuses on streamlined inventory.
Quality Assurance
Quality control checks at every stage of production help ensure that each product meets the required standards, enhancing customer satisfaction and reducing returns.4
Coordination with Suppliers and Downstream Partners
Coordination with suppliers and downstream supply chain partners is crucial for maintaining production schedules and ensuring the timely delivery of quality products. Clear communication, visibility across supply chain operations, and developing strong relationships with supply chain partners enhance the supply chain’s resilience and efficiency, paving the way for successful distribution.
Distributors: The Link
Acting as intermediaries between manufacturers and retailers, distributors facilitate the journey of finished goods from production to retail outlets. They often buy products in large quantities from multiple manufacturers, then manage and transport these products to retail outlets, helping manufacturers expand their reach into markets they might not otherwise serve due to logistical or cost constraints.5, 6
Working with Multiple Manufacturers Helps Distributors Provide Better Service
Distributors add value by handling complex supply chain tasks, including inventory management, order fulfillment, and shipping. Using their expertise and infrastructure to handle these tasks allows manufacturers to focus on creating value through production and product development.5 Distributors can also act as manufacturers' representatives by actively selling the manufacturer's products to purchasers downstream, educating resellers about product features and handling returns. Their localized presence and relationships with retailers allow for faster response times and flexible customer service.6
Distributors Add Value for Both the Manufacturer and the Retailer
Further, distributors provide additional services such as after-sales support and marketing to bridge the gap between manufacturers and the market. Their understanding of market dynamics, pricing, and customer preferences can help manufacturers adjust production plans and develop new products that meet market needs. Distributors can also bundle products from multiple manufacturers to provide custom solutions for purchasers.5, 6
By alleviating inventory and service burdens, distributors enable manufacturers to achieve better lead times and customer satisfaction. This collaboration often includes wholesalers, with whom manufacturers might also work to manage their supply chain efficiently.5, 6
Retail Outlets: The Connection
Retailers are the supply chain members that sell directly to most consumers. Retailers may be large chains or small independent businesses operating online e-commerce stores, brick-and-mortar stores, or a combination. A retailer might buy products from a distributor, a wholesaler, or both.
Effective retail strategies create customer demand for products, influence consumer purchase decisions and improve customer satisfaction. These strategies include advertising, merchandising, in-store promotions, pricing, customer service, and the overall consumer experience while shopping and buying products.7
Regarding SCM, retailers can be a valuable source of market research, influencing manufacturers' demand forecasting and inventory management. Because they work to understand consumer behavior and preferences so they can optimize their product offerings, retailers can also provide manufacturers with valuable feedback on why consumers buy products and on market trends. In turn, manufacturers can incorporate this feedback from the distribution channel into future production decisions.
End Customers: The Ultimate SCM Drivers
End customers are the ultimate drivers of the supply chain. Their demands dictate the flow and transformation of goods from suppliers to manufacturers and through distributors to retailers. When customer preferences and needs directly shape production strategies and inventory decisions, they contribute to the overall effectiveness of the supply chain.8
Customer demand determines what products are made, how they are packaged, the speed with which they need to be delivered, and the sustainability practices involved in their production and distribution. Manufacturers work to adjust their production volumes based on consumer trends and demands to avoid overproduction and underproduction. The former can lead to excess inventory costs and the latter to missed sales opportunities.8
Through buying products, end customers provide valuable data back to suppliers. Retailers and distributors gather customer feedback, sales data, and market trends and pass this information up the chain. Suppliers and manufacturers use this customer data to refine their processes and develop new products that better meet consumer needs.8
In addition to immediate purchasing decisions, customer behavior should drive broader supply chain strategies and innovations. A customer-centric approach includes strategies such as a demand-driven supply chain that uses real-time customer data to adjust production schedules and an omnichannel supply chain that coordinates inventory and fulfillment processes for a seamless customer experience.8, 9 These agile measures keep the supply chain responsive and efficient in meeting evolving marketplace needs.10
Become a Supply Chain Management Leader with Seattle University
Upgrade your career as a supply chain professional with Seattle University's Online MBA. The program includes a core course on Supply Chain Management, covering everything from procurement to inventory management and logistics. You can develop your expertise further with courses on Economics for Business, The Domestic and Global Economies, and Managerial Accounting for Organizational Leadership, which covers critical accounting practices that guide organizational decisions.
The curriculum also integrates Leadership Challenges that prepare you to face real-world situations with integrity and competence. These challenges simulate scenarios across business operations, positioning you to lead and innovate within complex supply chain operations.
By mastering these skills, you will be well-equipped to align your organization's strategies with end customer demands, driving success across all supply chain tiers. Contact an admissions outreach advisor today to learn more about how the Seattle University Online MBA can elevate your career.

By SAP Taulia

Share:
The supply chain process: Step by step
The supply chain process influences how effectively you manage costs, mitigate risks, and meet customer expectations. Learn how to optimize each stage here.
A guide to the supply chain process
An effective and efficient supply chain is vital to the success of any business. It contributes significantly to overall financial health, increases resilience against adverse conditions, and plays a vital role in ensuring products are delivered on time to customers.
By focusing on improving their supply chain processes, businesses can improve product quality, avoid inventory shortages or oversupply, increase customer satisfaction, protect against supply chain risks, and reduce costs.
Stages of the supply chain
A supply chain represents the flow of goods, materials, and services that underpin business operations, from sourcing raw materials to delivering finished goods. Each business has its own supply chain and is responsible for constructing and managing it to suit its unique objectives.
Involving suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, and customers, the supply chain process can be broken down into the following stages:
0. Establishing the supply chain
One of the most important steps in the supply chain process comes before it’s up and running. That step is planning and establishing a supply chain that is finely tuned to your specific business needs, the sector you operate in, and the market you serve.
During this step, you first need to decide on supply chain objectives and specify the metrics that will be used to monitor progress toward them. Then, move on to build your supply chain to suit those metrics. This includes supplier sourcing – the process of choosing suppliers to fulfil your need for goods and materials while also considering how well they serve your specific aims.
At this initial stage, you can also consider how to achieve visibility over inventory flowing through the supply chain, and determine how you’ll integrate other technological processes like cash flow and demand forecasting.
1. Purchasing materials/goods
With the sourcing of suppliers complete and the foundation of your supply chain well established, the supply chain process proper begins with purchasing raw materials and components.
The purchasing process formalizes the way in which a company buys goods and services, meaning that spending can be carefully managed and tracked. By having guidelines in place for every part of their purchasing process, from negotiating contracts to approving purchase requests, companies can ensure that their purchasing practices match their business objectives while increasing efficiency and minimizing risk.
2. Manufacturing and inventory management
The manufacturing process involves taking purchased raw materials and components and developing them into finished products. Although some manufacturing processes can be straightforward, modern manufacturing may include several steps that require products to pass through different facilities at various stages of completion.
At this stage, the priority is to make sure that the materials or components supplied meet the required standards. Rigorous measurement of supplier performance, in terms of order fulfillment rates, price accuracy, and quality of goods, act as metrics to assess them against. It’s also increasingly common to track the flow of goods and materials throughout these steps, to facilitate inventory visibility.
When complete, the final products must then be stored, so they’re ready for distribution to customers. Different options are available for this, each with their own pros and cons. The balance to be struck is between the cost of holding inventory and the speed with which you can fulfil orders.
Using inventory management techniques to oversee both raw materials and finished products, you can take steps to optimize stock levels and improve how well your inventory warehousing and product delivery process serves your business objectives.
3. Delivering products to customers
The distribution process involves the movement of finished products from storage to the end customer. Success here revolves around moving the right products, in the right quantity, on time, and to the correct location – all while managing costs.
Depending on your business goals and the market in which you operate, you can either deliver products directly to customers or distribute them indirectly through partners or third parties such as agents, wholesalers, or retailers.
Choices made at this stage of the supply chain process influence inventory cycle times and costs. Choosing the right distribution partners and tools can both drive efficiency and increase customer satisfaction levels.
4. Processing returns
Inevitably, there will be occasions when customers return products. When goods are returned, they need to be checked to see if they meet the criteria for returns and refunds. They are either repackaged for resale or disposed of, with the relevant data entered into an inventory system.
Refunds should be issued as quickly as possible, as customer relationships can be severely damaged if this process is mishandled. A smooth process, on the other hand, can strengthen customer relationships.
Optimizing your supply chain process
The supply chain process is a dynamic part of business operations, each stage of which can be optimized and refined to bring about efficiency. These are some of the most viable methods for improving the way your supply chain operates.
Choosing the right supply chain model
First and foremost, it’s essential that you adopt a supply chain model that fits both your unique objectives and the sector in which you operate.
The main dichotomy in supply chain models is between lean and resilient. Lean supply chains seek to eliminate unnecessary expenditure, turning raw materials into finished goods with minimal waste and loss. Resilient supply chains are designed to adapt quickly to unanticipated events by holding safety stock and having a degree of supplier redundancy built in. The former approach is geared to ‘just-in-time’ inventory practices, and the latter ‘just-in-case’.
The lean approach relies on all the supply chain steps functioning almost perfectly. Without safety stock, disruption from a single supplier could cause production to halt almost immediately. On the other hand, a resilient approach will come with greater storage costs and a greater risk of obsolescence while tying up more cash.
Refining your approach to supply chain management
Efficient and effective supply chain management should include all the different supply chain steps, including sourcing and selecting suppliers, procurement, supplier management, inventory tracking, and the delivery of finished products or goods.
Supply chain management (SCM) software can help in the planning, execution, and tracking of operations, thereby streamlining the entire process. SCM software can coordinate and consolidate these various supply chain steps by addressing the discrete stages of supply chain management, such as sourcing and development, ordering, production, inventory, financial management, and distribution.
By enabling different departments to work together and make better-informed decisions, SCM software can also help businesses reduce costs, increase profitability, and maximize their working capital.
Running regular risk assessments
The different stages of the supply chain present various risks, which can arise due to internal and external factors.
Internal risks include poorly planned processes, inadequate forecasting capabilities, disruptions such as staff shortages and software issues, and a lack of contingency planning. External supply chain risks, meanwhile, can include problems with suppliers, shortages of raw materials, demand volatility, and environmental and political disruption.
By establishing a thorough risk assessment procedure and analyzing supply chain risks regularly, a business can identify potential problems before they arise. Companies will therefore be better placed to mitigate any operational challenges – from major supply chain breakdowns to minor disruptions.

Market Leaders Take Control of the 5 Phases in Supply Chain Management
Monday, July 22, 2024
Supply chain store attendant
Are you struggling to streamline your supply chain operations? Discover how market leaders master the five phases of supply chain management to stay ahead of the competition and enhance efficiency
Supply chain management (SCM) is crucial for small-to-midsize businesses focused on efficiency and market competitiveness. Here, we detail the five pivotal phases of effective SCM—planning, sourcing, manufacturing, delivering, and returning—and take a look at how SCM has evolved in response to globalization, technological advancements, and changing consumer expectations, making effective management even more important.
What is supply chain management and why is it important?
SCM focuses on making the journey of products from creation to customer as smooth and cost-effective as possible. This process includes everything from the start of making a product to developing it and using technology to keep track of everything. SCM helps businesses oversee production, shipping, and distribution in a way that cuts down on unnecessary costs and speeds up how quickly products reach buyers. By carefully managing what's kept in stock, how things are made, distributed, and sold, and even keeping an eye on what suppliers have, companies can be more efficient.
Taking control of our SCM processes involves recognizing that almost every product we sell is the result of a team effort by several companies working together. Although this idea has been around for a long time, it’s only recently that businesses have started to see managing this process well as a key competitive advantage. By getting rid of waste and focusing on what customers want, effective SCM saves money and helps companies deliver more value to their customers. It's about constantly finding ways to do things better.
How has the importance of supply chain management evolved in recent years?
Exploring the shifting landscape of supply chain management over time reveals a dynamic evolution in its importance, driven by changes in the business environment and in technology.
Globalization
In the 2000s and 2010s, globalization changed how companies manage their supply chains because of the increase in global trade and the opening of new markets, especially in Asia. This change meant companies could get materials from more places and sell products to more customers worldwide. But, managing a supply chain became more complicated because companies had to move goods across different countries and understand each area's ways of doing things. This period was crucial for companies to find new ways to keep their supply chains efficient, despite these challenges.
Consumer expectations and desire for more transparency
The evolution of SCM towards greater transparency and responsiveness to consumer expectations traces back to the late 20th century when operational efficiency and cost reduction were the primary focuses. As globalization expanded and consumers became more informed, there was a growing demand for not just quality and affordability, but also ethical practices and sustainability. This shift was further amplified by technological advancements, enabling real-time tracking and transparency in sourcing. The rise of the digital age and social media has also played a pivotal role, giving consumers a powerful voice to influence brand reputations and demand accountability.
Technological advancements
Technological advancements have been reshaping SCM by making data handling more efficient. Cloud-based solutions revolutionize how data is stored and shared, enabling real-time collaboration across the supply chain and streamlining decision-making and operations. Big data analytics turns the vast volumes of generated data into actionable insights, helping to predict demand fluctuations and optimize processes for cost reduction and improved efficiency.
The integration of Internet of Things (IoT) devices provides immediate insights into goods' locations and conditions, optimizing inventory and logistics. Artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) automate and refine forecasting and operational decisions, enhancing supply chain performance. Blockchain technology introduces secure, transparent record-keeping, vital for product traceability and compliance. Meanwhile, edge computing processes data closer to its source, speeding up response times for critical decisions, and data visualization tools simplify complex data analysis and communication, supporting quick, informed decision-making.
Improving the 5 key phases of supply chain management
For small-to-midsize business owners and executives, mastering and taking control of the five key areas of Supply Chain Management (SCM) is essential for improving efficiency and gaining a competitive edge. Here’s how each phase can be enhanced:
1. Planning
Planning is the strategic phase where supply chain efficiency begins. Accurate forecasting of future demands is crucial, as it influences every subsequent step—from sourcing to production to delivery. Utilizing ERP systems in this phase allows businesses to integrate data from various sources, providing a holistic view of operations. These systems help identify trends, predict demand spikes, and prepare for potential disruptions by aligning supply with manufacturing and customer needs. For SMBs, this means being able to compete on agility and responsiveness, planning not just for the immediate future but also setting the stage for long-term growth.
2. Sourcing
Sourcing requires painstaking selection and management of suppliers to ensure a consistent, high-quality supply of materials at competitive prices. Beyond basic vendor selection, effective sourcing involves:
ERP systems play a crucial role here by facilitating supplier evaluation, managing contracts, and tracking compliance with sustainability standards. This comprehensive approach to sourcing allows for a more dynamic and responsive supply chain capable of adapting to market changes and challenges.
3. Manufacturing
Manufacturing, the core of the supply chain, transforms raw materials into finished goods through a series of carefully managed steps, including assembly, testing, and packaging. Efficiency and quality control are vital to minimize waste and ensure the final product meets specifications. ERP systems enhance this phase by:
They provide real-time visibility into the manufacturing process, allowing for quick adjustments to avoid delays and reduce downtime. This means greater control over production, the ability to respond swiftly to quality issues, and the flexibility to adapt to new opportunities or challenges in product development.
4. Delivering
Delivering focuses on getting the finished product to the customer efficiently and reliably. It encompasses order processing, warehousing, transportation, and customer service. ERP solutions can significantly improve this phase by:
They also support customer service by providing accurate, up-to-date information on order status and inventory levels. For SMBs, effective delivery is a key differentiator, directly impacting customer satisfaction and loyalty.
5. Returning
Returning involves managing returns and exchanges, an often overlooked but critical aspect of SCM. Effective management requires a process for handling returned goods, assessing their condition, and reintegrating them into inventory if applicable. ERP systems aid in this process by:
This phase is also an opportunity for feedback, allowing businesses to identify product or delivery issues.
Conclusion
Mastering the five key phases of supply chain management and adapting to changes brought by global trends, customer demands, and technological advancements allows businesses to improve operations and stand out in the competitive market. As SCM keeps changing, businesses that stay flexible and embrace innovation will find themselves leading their markets, ready to meet the challenges and opportunities of tomorrow. By adopting these proven strategies, your business can achieve greater efficiency and customer satisfaction. Start optimizing your supply chain today to reap the benefits.
Alan Margulis
By Alan Margulis
Alan Margulis is an accomplished copywriter with two decades of experience in content marketing, nurture stream, and direct response writing. He has done extensive work in a wide range of industries, from software and academia to staffing and entrepreneurial.

关于佳福

佳福（福建）染整有限公司成立于2012 年，隶属于三福（中国）集团旗下，现有 员工1000余人。引进高效、节能、环保的 染整设备，被评为泉州市“智能制造数字 化示范车间”；通过ISO9001\ISO14001\OHSAS18001等质量、环境、职业健康 安全等管理体系；通过了国际OEKOTEX ®STANDARD 100、BLUESIGN®认证和 GRS认证，检测中心获国家合格评定认可 实验室，使产品在研发、采购、生产、检测 的过程中符合绿色环保要求。
佳福注重产品研发和流行趋势开发，多次 荣获国家级奖项，如“ 中国时尚面料入围 企业”、“优质化纤面料金奖”等国家级奖 项。
佳福注重环境保护与绿色可持续发展，先 后被评为生态治理先进单位、福建省级绿 色工厂、全国纺织行业绿色发展节水型企 业；
为什么可持续发展对供应商很重要？

随着环境问题成为人们关注的焦点，品牌、监管机构和消费者都要求供应商提高透明度，承担更大的责任。但这对服装和纺织行业的供应商意味着什么？
数据表明：
70%的品牌更喜欢拥有透明的可持续发展数据的供应商。品牌正在优先考虑那些能够提供可验证数据的供应商。如果没有透明度，供应商就有可能把业务输给已经准备好的竞争对手。
时尚供应链占全球碳排放量的10%。服装业是造成气候变化的最大因素之一。减少碳排放不再仅仅是合规性的问题，而是关于在一个可持续性是品牌和消费者的关键决策因素的市场中保持相关性。。
纺织生产占全球工业水污染的20%。纺织制造中的化学密集型工艺造成了严重的水污染。品牌越来越多地执行更严格的环境要求，这使得供应商必须改善废水管理和化学品合规性。
CleanChain如何赋能供应商？
供应商需要合适的工具来应对这些挑战并实现可持续发展目标。CleanChain简化了环境合规和可持续发展报告，帮助供应商
✅自动化合规性追踪，并确保符合ZDHC MRSL和其他法规。
✅通过实时数据洞察和性能监控减少碳和水足迹。
✅改善化学品管理，确保更安全、更可持续的生产过程。
✅通过提供经过验证的、透明的可持续发展数据，与品牌建立信任。
可持续供应链的未来
可持续性不仅仅是满足法规要求——它还关乎提高竞争优势，加强品牌关系，以及企业的未来发展。随着对可持续发展的期望不断提高，主动适应的供应商将最有利于长期成功。
cleanchain.cn@adec-innovations.com
东丽化学创新 除了CleanChain的功能优势之外，它还帮助用户简化了与电子表格相关的复杂性操作。 关于东丽酒伊织染(南通)有限公司

东丽酒伊织染 (南通) 有限公司 (公司简称 TSD), 成立于1994年, 是东丽集团 (Toray) 在中国投资规模最大的制造型公司, 是一家以化学合成纤维为主的坯布织造、功能性面料加工·染色、成衣制造销售及水处理 为核心事业的公司。公司拥有从新技术研 发、织造/染色/后整理/检测及成衣制 造的一条龙生产流程。作为东丽海外的标 杆工厂, TSD拥有一流的安全、环境和职业 卫生、能源管理体系, 践行着TSD对于社会 责任感的承诺。公司秉承“通过创造新的 价值为社会做贡献”的企业理念, 以不懈的 创新精神和科技实力为客户不断开发品质 上乘、性能卓越的面料, 谋求与每一位顾客 的共同发展。
客户面临的挑战
在采用CleanChain这款在线化学品管理系统之前, 我们在执行ZDHC的过程中, 由于化学品使用类别多且量大, 很难实现实时追踪现有化学品的MRSL合规性。同时, 针对没有合规性的化学品以及证书到期的产品, 我们需要人工核实和整理相关列表, 并一一和化学品制剂商进行沟通。整个过程需要花费大量的时间，极大地影响我们的工作效率。另外, 如何提高MRLS的整体符合性，也是我们的一大挑战。最后, 在采用系统前, 我们不明确我司客户对于我们进入CleanChain平台持何种态度及其认可程度如何。
CleanChain解决方案
我司化学品管理工作者每月在系统里按时上传化学品清单，并下载InCheck报告。为了避免用户错过上传的时间截点, CleanChain还会有自动化的邮件提醒用户及时上传化学品数据。除了定期上传化学品数据外, 我们日常工作中,也会利用系统的Dashboard来查看到期的产品以及没有合规性的产品列表。根据这份列表, 我们有针对性地和化学品供应商开展高效的沟通, 鼓励并帮助他们对未合规的产品进行检测并上传至ZDHC Gateway网关。同时, 在数据的分享上, 通过CleanChain的connect功能, 与客户取得关联, 系统可自动帮助用户将CIL数据和InCheck报告分享给我们的合作品牌。CleanChain在数据的管理上, 帮助我们节省了手动分享报告和清单的时间, 大大地提高了工作效率 。
CleanChain带给我们的价值
采用CleanChain系统，在很大程度上帮助我司规避了化学品的风险物质, 也大大提高了我司化学品管理方向的工作效率。同时, CleanChain系统的采用提升了客户对于我司的认可度及信任度, 尤其是对于了解或者已经使用CleanChain平台的客户而言。最后, CleanChain促进了我司可持续发展进程。

The Four Main Supply Chain Models

While retail businesses, manufacturers, and other organizations depend on various factors for their success, choosing the right type of supply chain might be the most integral. Supply chains are systems of logistics that include every step necessary for converting a raw material to a finished product ready for consumer use. Entire teams are devoted to managing these phases and all the processes involved so that businesses can sell their products at a profit.
However, one type of supply chain doesn’t necessarily fit all organizations. Different supply chain models are used for different types of products or services to meet various customer needs. An efficient supply chain model can help an organization’s business operations reach or exceed their goals and keep consumers satisfied.
Read on to learn more about the different supply chain models, how they compare, and how to choose the right one for your business.
Understanding Supply Chain Models
There are four main supply chain models in use today: the continuous-flow model, fast model, efficient model, and custom-configured model. Each model plays a specific role in managing and optimizing the flow of a business’s products or services. Supply chain management in general aims to keep costs low, inventory levels stable, and delays minimized, and each model approaches these goals in a different way.
Understanding and implementing the right supply chain model can have a considerable impact on an organization’s bottom line. For example, a designer clothes manufacturer using a continuous-flow model might not be as effective as one using a fast model, as fashion trends come and go quickly. Both company and consumer benefit the most from the supply chain model that meets their unique specifications.
The Importance of Supply Chain Models
The overall efficiency and cost of a supply chain largely hinge on the type of model employed. Many moving parts make up a supply chain, including initial planning and budgeting, acquisition and storage of raw materials, manufacturing of goods, shipping and delivery, and the final sale of finished products. Each of the four supply chain models are designed to optimize these stages depending on the types of products made and their intended market.
A particularly slow turnaround time in the supply chain may hurt the organization’s financial well-being. Similarly, low-quality products or services might cause customers to take their business elsewhere. That’s why proper supply chain strategy is an important first step to identify which model will best serve the entire chain.
The Continuous-Flow Model
Many industries call for constant use and replenishment of specific products or services. These industries include pharmaceuticals, automotive manufacturing, food service, and more. Businesses and organizations involved in these types of supply chains typically adopt the continuous-flow model for maximum efficiency.
Perhaps the most traditional of the supply chain models, the continuous-flow model works well for organizations with high-volume production lines and mostly uniform goods. This model aims to keep each stage of the supply chain moving at a balanced, streamlined pace. Supply chain managers using the continuous-flow model typically emphasize high efficiency, standardization of operations, and quality control. Continuous-flow supply chains pair well with a stable demand for the products and services involved.
The Fast Model
Fast models are useful for businesses that, while still engaging in all the standard phases of the supply chain, need to keep up with shifting consumer demand. These businesses manufacture finished products that tend to have a short market life cycle. Fashion brands, electronics companies, and other consumer goods organizations benefit from the fast model since their products may be perceived as trendy and experience quick turnaround periods.
But it’s not always about trendiness. Many other businesses use the fast model if they focus on speed and responsiveness. Fast model strategies usually seek to maximize returns, minimize delivery times, and keep customers happy with their purchases. Product quality isn’t necessarily ignored, but it tends not to be the highest priority in this model.
The Efficient Model
Competitive industries stick to the efficient model so that their products and services can reach the market as quickly as possible, especially when demand is forecasted to be high. Since efficiency is their main goal, organizations using this model make sure to prepare raw materials and manufacturing processes well in advance to avoid delays.
Supply chain managers who operate under the efficient model keep a keen eye on inventory management and strive to get the most output from production equipment and labor. Additionally, these managers work to balance low costs with good product quality. The types of organizations that use the efficient model vary widely and may include anything from breakfast cereal manufacturers to asphalt companies.
The Custom-Configured Model
Some organizations deal with very specific types of products or have a customer base with unique needs. These organizations fare well with a custom-configured supply chain model. As its name implies, the custom-configured model is designed for customized scenarios in material acquisition, production assembly, and delivery of finished goods. This includes prototype products or products with multiple configurations. A prosthetics manufacturer, for instance, might use the custom-configured model to create and ship their products to hospitals or specialty care clinics.
More flexibility and, in some cases, more investment may be required under this model. Speed is not as important as careful, methodical management of the supply chain, often with specific customer input. Common examples of organizations that use the custom-configured model include furniture stores, computer manufacturers, and makers of musical instruments.
Choosing the Right Supply Chain Model
Selecting the appropriate model for any given supply chain should be a top priority for supply chain leaders and strategists. These professionals should base their choice on the nature of the industry, customer demand, and their overall business goals. But if you help oversee your organization’s supply chain, how exactly do you make the right choice?
First, understand the importance of being adaptable and flexible. Sometimes, businesses need to be prepared to switch models or adapt their existing one in response to market fluctuations. But even in a perfect market landscape, you should be aware of how each model works and tailor a model to your specific supply chain. Ask yourself what types of products or services you’re providing, what kinds of consumers are buying them, and how much your organization is willing to spend throughout each stage of the supply chain.
Leveraging Supply Chain Models for Competitive Advantage
Using the right supply chain model can potentially transform a business’s operations, improving efficiency and providing a competitive advantage. But being willing to switch, adjust, or reassess models when needed is just as important as using the right one. Fortunately, supply chain leaders don’t need to navigate these models alone, as advancements in technology like AI, machine learning, and blockchain are influencing how supply chains are managed. Dedicated software programs can also help determine the best supply chain models and aid in supervising the stages of the chain.
Next Steps
Planning out your ideal career in supply chain management? Consider studying at WGU, where you can enroll in an online, accredited bachelor’s program in business management or supply chain and operations management. Flexible programs like these can provide the skills you need to implement the best supply chain model for your organization. Plus, you can study at your own pace, with no set log-in times for coursework.
Our business programs are designed with input by industry experts and can be completed as fast as you master the material, so that you can potentially save time and money on your way to your dream supply chain management job. Get started today!

Understanding the Generic Supply Chain: From Raw Materials to End Consumers
Every product we use follows a journey—one that begins with sourcing raw materials and ends in the hands of the consumer. This journey is the supply chain, and here's how it flows:
1. Raw Material Sourcing & Extraction
2. Logistics to Suppliers (Wholesalers)
3. Manufacturing & Refining into Finished Goods
4. Distribution to Retailers
5. Retail Sales to Consumers
But it doesn’t end there. Consumer demand loops back, influencing future production and driving the entire cycle forward.
Whether you're in procurement, manufacturing, or logistics—understanding this cycle is key to building efficient and responsive supply chains.
#SupplyChain #Logistics #Procurement #Manufacturing #BusinessOperations #SupplyChainManagement #SCM #Distribution #Retail #ConsumerDemand

Purchasing vs Procurement vs Supply Chain - What's the Real Difference?
In the world of global trade and business operations, these three terms - Purchasing, Procurement, and Supply Chain are often used interchangeably. But in reality, they play very different roles in driving an organization's success. Let's break it down
Purchasing
This is the transactional stage where businesses buy goods or services, issue purchase orders (POs), and make payments. It's all about execution - ensuring the right product is bought at the right price and delivered on time.
Procurement
Procurement goes a level deeper. It's a strategic process involving supplier sourcing, contract negotiation, and vendor management. The goal here is not just to buy but to build long-term partnerships, ensure quality assurance, and achieve cost efficiency through smart decision-making.
Supply Chain
This is the backbone of business logistics - covering transportation, warehousing, and distribution. The supply chain ensures products move efficiently from vendors to customers, creating a seamless flow of goods, information, and value.
#SupplyChain #Procurement #Purchasing #BusinessOperations #Logistics #VendorManagement #StrategicSourcing #OperationalExcellence #BusinessGrowth #SmartOperations #GlobalTrade #FMCG #SupplyChainManagement #ProcurementStrategy #EfficiencyMatters

Many people see supply chain as just logistics or procurement — but in reality, it’s the connective thread that ties every factor of a business together.
🔗 Suppliers – sourcing raw materials
🔗 Procurement – negotiating and securing resources
🔗 Production/Operations – turning inputs into products
🔗 Warehousing & Inventory – storing and balancing stock
🔗 Logistics & Transportation – moving goods efficiently
🔗 Sales & Marketing – ensuring product availability for customers
🔗 Customer Service – delivering on promises and building trust
From the first handshake with a supplier to the moment a product reaches the customer’s hands — supply chain integrates all functions into one seamless system.
In short: No Supply Chain = No Business Flow.
#SupplyChain #BusinessExcellence #Logistics #Procurement #Operations #CustomerSuccess

To view or add a comment, sign in

Insight from the Center for Procurement, Logistics, and Supply Chain Management.
Logistics focuses on the movement, storage, and flow of goods, services, and information within a specific organization or segment of the supply chain. It ensures that the right product reaches the right place, at the right time, in the right condition, and at the right cost.
Supply Chain Management (SCM), on the other hand, is broader and strategic. It integrates all activities—from raw material sourcing to final product delivery—across multiple organizations, aligning suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, and retailers under a coordinated value network.
In essence:
“Logistics delivers the product.
Supply Chain delivers the promise.”
Both are interdependent — logistics provides the execution muscle, while supply chain management provides the strategic mind.

In today’s fast-paced business landscape, these terms are often used interchangeably, but they play unique roles in driving success:
1️⃣ Purchasing: The basic act of buying, think POs and payments. It’s essential but often lacks strategic depth.
2️⃣ Procurement: A step beyond purchasing, focusing on sourcing, contracts, and vendor management. It adds value by fostering beneficial supplier relationships and enhancing cost effectiveness.
3️⃣ Supply Chain: The broader ecosystem encompassing logistics, warehousing, and distribution.
It ensures that the right products reach the right customers at the right time.

Strong procurement and supply chain practices lead to impactful outcomes:
•Cost Savings: Streamlining processes reduces unnecessary expenses.
•Sustainability: Thoughtful sourcing and logistics can minimize environmental footprints.
Understanding these differences enables organizations to implement strategies that maximize efficiency and drive growth.

Get a quote
Mar 13, 2025
How a Supply Chain Works: The Journey of Products from Start to Finish
Back to posts
Share
#### Get a Quote
In today’s interconnected world, we often don’t stop to think about how products end up in our hands. From the food we eat to the gadgets we use, everything we buy comes from a complex network of businesses, activities, and processes known as a supply chain. Whether it’s a local business or a multinational corporation, the flow of goods from raw materials to final consumers relies on an efficient and well-organized supply chain.
But how does a supply chain actually work? In this blog, we’ll break it down step by step and highlight why each part of the process is crucial for getting products to market.
Raw Materials & Suppliers
The journey of any product begins with raw materials. These are the basic components used to create a product, such as steel, cotton, plastic, or minerals. The first stage of the supply chain involves suppliers, who provide these materials to manufacturers. For example, in the clothing industry, suppliers might provide fabric or threads to a manufacturer. In the electronics industry, a supplier could provide microchips or metals needed to assemble devices. Suppliers may be located nearby or across the world, depending on the nature of the raw materials and the product being created. The challenge here is to ensure that these materials are available in the right quantities and at the right time.
Manufacturing
Once the raw materials arrive, the next step in the supply chain is manufacturing. This is the process where raw materials are turned into finished products. Manufacturing could happen in a factory, a production plant, or a facility designed for assembly. As products are made, they are often packaged and prepared for shipment to the next step in the supply chain.
Warehousing and Storage
After manufacturing, the products don’t always go directly to customers. They typically go to warehouses or distribution centers, where they are stored until they are needed. Warehouses serve as holding areas for products before they are sent out to retailers or other destinations. Think of a warehouse as a big “middle stop” for products, where they are organized, inventoried, and prepared for transport. The challenge here is to keep track of inventory and ensure that there is enough stock to meet demand without overstocking, which could result in excess storage costs.
Freight and Transportation
This is where freight transportation plays a critical role. Moving products from warehouses to their final destinations is a key part of the supply chain. Freight transportation involves several methods of transport, including:
 Road freight: Using trucks to transport goods between locations. It’s flexible and can be used for both short and long distances.
 Rail freight: Often used for large or bulk shipments over longer distances, especially in regions with extensive rail networks.
 Sea freight: Cargo ships transport goods internationally across oceans. This is typically used for large, bulk shipments, such as containers of consumer goods.
 Air freight: The fastest mode of transportation, used for high-value, time-sensitive goods like electronics or medical supplies.
Choosing the best transport method is critical for minimizing costs and ensuring timely deliveries.
Retailers and Distribution
Once the products are in transit and arrive at retail or distribution centers, they are ready to be sold to the end consumer. Retailers could be physical stores, e-commerce websites, or even wholesalers who distribute goods to smaller businesses. The key here is ensuring that the right products reach the right retailers at the right time. Retailers often need information from suppliers about stock levels, lead times, and shipping schedules.
End Customer
Finally, we reach the customer — the person or business that buys and uses the product. At this stage, the success of the supply chain is measured by customer satisfaction. If products are delivered on time, in good condition, and at the right price, the supply chain has done its job.
Challenges in the Supply Chain
Even though the supply chain may seem straightforward, there are several challenges that companies face:
 Delays and disruptions: Weather events, transportation issues, or supply shortages can cause delays that impact the entire supply chain.
 Cost fluctuations: Changes in fuel prices, labor costs, or raw material prices can increase the cost of production and transportation.
 Inventory management: Ensuring that the right amount of stock is available — not too little and not too much — can be tricky. Poor inventory management can lead to stockouts or overstock, both of which are costly.
Why Does a Supply Chain Matter?
An efficient supply chain is critical to the success of any business. A well-organized supply chain ensures that products reach customers quickly, reduce costs, and improve profitability. On the flip side, a broken or inefficient supply chain can cause delays, increase costs, and lead to poor customer satisfaction.
In a globalized world, supply chains are more complex than ever, with goods crossing borders and going through multiple stages of production. But no matter the scale, the goal remains the same: to deliver products to customers in the most efficient, cost-effective way possible.
From raw materials to the final customer, the supply chain is the backbone of the global economy. By understanding how it works and its various components, businesses can improve their efficiency, cut costs, and better meet customer demand. Whether you’re a large corporation or a small business, optimizing your supply chain is crucial for success in today’s competitive market.
If you’re looking for ways to improve your supply chain, transportation is a key piece of the puzzle. Easley Transportation specializes in efficient, reliable transportation solutions that help move products smoothly from point A to point B. Contact us today to learn how we can help streamline your supply chain!

Seattle University
Understanding the Key Elements of Supply Chain Management (SCM)
illustration of global locations in a supply chain
As the labor shortages and pandemic closings of recent years have shown, interruptions in the supply chain can easily cause chaos in the economy. Modern business depends on a resilient global supply chain to function effectively.1
In a manufacturing supply chain, suppliers provide raw materials to manufacturers who produce goods. These finished goods are sent to distributors, who handle logistics and inventory management. Retailers purchase products from distributors to sell to end customers, the final link in the supply chain.
Beginning with the supplier, each link in the chain has to perform its role well to support profitable operations for the business and satisfaction for the end customer.2 Supply chain management professionals can enhance operations for each supply chain member organization. This article will explore how today's supply chain participants work together for smooth business operations.
Suppliers: The Foundation of SCM
Suppliers are key players in the supply chain who provide the raw materials and fabricated components manufacturers need to make products. Whether it’s through high-quality raw materials or defect-free components, suppliers' quality directly influences the final product's quality. When they deliver materials that meet specifications on time, suppliers help manufacturers meet production schedules, maintain consistency in lead times, and achieve customer satisfaction.3
As the first link in the chain, suppliers can significantly contribute to their customers' success in various ways. In addition to supporting cost management by delivering reliably, they can help mitigate risks by providing alternative sources during disruptions. Suppliers can enhance the brand's reputation when they cater to environmental and ethical considerations and practice sustainable sourcing.3
Strong Relationships with Suppliers Support Resilient Supply Chains
Building strong supplier relationships is essential for supply chain success, and you can use several tactics to build a strong supplier network. Begin with the procurement process, choosing reliable suppliers through thorough research and audits. Clear communication about expectations and regular performance reviews are crucial. Treat suppliers as valued partners and invest time developing long-term relationships based on trust and mutual benefits. This collaboration can lead to strategic partnerships creating a competitive advantage for both the manufacturer and the supplier by speeding up the time to market and enhancing product quality.3
However, given the complexities of the global market, manufacturers need to build diversified supplier networks for a reliable and responsive supply base. Building long-term relationships with diverse suppliers can help manufacturers guarantee a steady flow of materials and respond quickly to market changes and disruptions.3
Manufacturers: The Powerhouse
Manufacturers play a central role in the supply chain by converting raw materials into finished goods. Their efficiency and quality influence overall business success, impacting costs, product quality, and customer satisfaction. Finely tuned operations can enhance lead times and achieve cost management goals. Effective management of several interconnected processes is integral to successful manufacturing operations.4
Demand Forecasting
Accurate demand forecasting helps manufacturers determine how much raw material to procure and when to start production. This ensures optimal resource allocation and reduces the risk of overproduction or stockouts.4
Receiving
Receiving involves inspecting delivered materials to ensure they meet quality standards and match purchase orders, safeguarding subsequent production phases from substandard inputs.4
Inventory Management
Efficient inventory management tracks inventory through the production process from raw materials to finished goods, ensuring materials are available when needed without overstocking or running short and holding up production.4 Just-in-Time (JIT) is a lean manufacturing approach that focuses on streamlined inventory.
Quality Assurance
Quality control checks at every stage of production help ensure that each product meets the required standards, enhancing customer satisfaction and reducing returns.4
Coordination with Suppliers and Downstream Partners
Coordination with suppliers and downstream supply chain partners is crucial for maintaining production schedules and ensuring the timely delivery of quality products. Clear communication, visibility across supply chain operations, and developing strong relationships with supply chain partners enhance the supply chain’s resilience and efficiency, paving the way for successful distribution.
Distributors: The Link
Acting as intermediaries between manufacturers and retailers, distributors facilitate the journey of finished goods from production to retail outlets. They often buy products in large quantities from multiple manufacturers, then manage and transport these products to retail outlets, helping manufacturers expand their reach into markets they might not otherwise serve due to logistical or cost constraints.5, 6
Working with Multiple Manufacturers Helps Distributors Provide Better Service
Distributors add value by handling complex supply chain tasks, including inventory management, order fulfillment, and shipping. Using their expertise and infrastructure to handle these tasks allows manufacturers to focus on creating value through production and product development.5 Distributors can also act as manufacturers' representatives by actively selling the manufacturer's products to purchasers downstream, educating resellers about product features and handling returns. Their localized presence and relationships with retailers allow for faster response times and flexible customer service.6
Distributors Add Value for Both the Manufacturer and the Retailer
Further, distributors provide additional services such as after-sales support and marketing to bridge the gap between manufacturers and the market. Their understanding of market dynamics, pricing, and customer preferences can help manufacturers adjust production plans and develop new products that meet market needs. Distributors can also bundle products from multiple manufacturers to provide custom solutions for purchasers.5, 6
By alleviating inventory and service burdens, distributors enable manufacturers to achieve better lead times and customer satisfaction. This collaboration often includes wholesalers, with whom manufacturers might also work to manage their supply chain efficiently.5, 6
Retail Outlets: The Connection
Retailers are the supply chain members that sell directly to most consumers. Retailers may be large chains or small independent businesses operating online e-commerce stores, brick-and-mortar stores, or a combination. A retailer might buy products from a distributor, a wholesaler, or both.
Effective retail strategies create customer demand for products, influence consumer purchase decisions and improve customer satisfaction. These strategies include advertising, merchandising, in-store promotions, pricing, customer service, and the overall consumer experience while shopping and buying products.7
Regarding SCM, retailers can be a valuable source of market research, influencing manufacturers' demand forecasting and inventory management. Because they work to understand consumer behavior and preferences so they can optimize their product offerings, retailers can also provide manufacturers with valuable feedback on why consumers buy products and on market trends. In turn, manufacturers can incorporate this feedback from the distribution channel into future production decisions.
End Customers: The Ultimate SCM Drivers
End customers are the ultimate drivers of the supply chain. Their demands dictate the flow and transformation of goods from suppliers to manufacturers and through distributors to retailers. When customer preferences and needs directly shape production strategies and inventory decisions, they contribute to the overall effectiveness of the supply chain.8
Customer demand determines what products are made, how they are packaged, the speed with which they need to be delivered, and the sustainability practices involved in their production and distribution. Manufacturers work to adjust their production volumes based on consumer trends and demands to avoid overproduction and underproduction. The former can lead to excess inventory costs and the latter to missed sales opportunities.8
Through buying products, end customers provide valuable data back to suppliers. Retailers and distributors gather customer feedback, sales data, and market trends and pass this information up the chain. Suppliers and manufacturers use this customer data to refine their processes and develop new products that better meet consumer needs.8
In addition to immediate purchasing decisions, customer behavior should drive broader supply chain strategies and innovations. A customer-centric approach includes strategies such as a demand-driven supply chain that uses real-time customer data to adjust production schedules and an omnichannel supply chain that coordinates inventory and fulfillment processes for a seamless customer experience.8, 9 These agile measures keep the supply chain responsive and efficient in meeting evolving marketplace needs.10
Become a Supply Chain Management Leader with Seattle University
Upgrade your career as a supply chain professional with Seattle University's Online MBA. The program includes a core course on Supply Chain Management, covering everything from procurement to inventory management and logistics. You can develop your expertise further with courses on Economics for Business, The Domestic and Global Economies, and Managerial Accounting for Organizational Leadership, which covers critical accounting practices that guide organizational decisions.
The curriculum also integrates Leadership Challenges that prepare you to face real-world situations with integrity and competence. These challenges simulate scenarios across business operations, positioning you to lead and innovate within complex supply chain operations.
By mastering these skills, you will be well-equipped to align your organization's strategies with end customer demands, driving success across all supply chain tiers. Contact an admissions outreach advisor today to learn more about how the Seattle University Online MBA can elevate your career.

metadata.title: What is supply chain? - McKinsey

documents.page_content:
EasyPost Blog
Carrier Network, EasyPost Enterprise, Tracking API
Supply Chain Process: From Planning to Returns
Jaidyn Farar
Jaidyn Farar
January 30, 2025
10 min read
When every step of the supply chain process runs smoothly, your business will gain and retain customers more easily. On the other hand, delays and mistakes tend to frustrate shoppers—and convince them to turn to competitors.
In this article, you’ll learn how to keep each link of your supply chain efficient and cost-effective.
Introduction to the supply chain process
Supply chain, supply chain process, and supply chain management; while these terms are closely related, they all have distinct meanings. Let’s walk through each one.
What is a supply chain?
Supply chains are networks of businesses, individuals, and processes involved in creating products and delivering them to end customers. These networks are made up of raw material suppliers, manufacturers, transportation companies, wholesalers, retailers, and customers.
The goal of every supply chain is to make products available in the right quantity, at the right time, and at the right location.
What is the supply chain process?
The supply chain process is the series of steps required to move goods from their point of origin to the customer. This includes planning, procurement, manufacturing, inventory management, distribution, and returns processing.
By streamlining each stage of the process, from sourcing materials to managing last-mile delivery, you’ll keep your operations efficient and your customers satisfied.
What is supply chain management?
Supply chain management (SCM) is the practice of overseeing and optimizing the entire supply chain process. This includes managing relationships with suppliers, monitoring inventory, coordinating logistics, and leveraging technology. Businesses that take a proactive approach to SCM are often able to minimize the impact of disruptions and build strong relationships with their supply chain partners.
Stages in the Supply Chain Process
The six stages of the supply chain process are planning, procurement, manufacturing, inventory management, distribution, and returns processing.
After reading about each stage, check out the linked resources for more information, including tips from expert guests on the Unboxing Logistics podcast.
1. Planning
Planning is the foundation of the supply chain process, and it all starts with demand forecasting.
Before manufacturing and distributing products, you need to know how many you can expect to sell. Otherwise, you might stock out of popular items or end up with warehouse shelves full of dead stock.
Demand forecasting is the process of predicting how much product will be needed over a given period. For the most accurate forecasts, you should analyze both internal and external factors. In addition to using historical sales data to predict future sales, remember to consider things like customer behavior, market trends, and competitor activity.
A solid supply chain plan should also outline procurement strategies, production schedules, and logistics timelines. Just remember to build flexibility into your plan to account for unexpected disruptions, such as supplier delays or spikes in demand.
> “The biggest thing that an organization can do to be resilient is to improve the quality of the people and get them thinking in a resilient mindset. You don’t have to train for every potential type of disaster or event. It’s the idea of being able to react to events, process them, set up the communication, make decisions.” — Chris Caplice, Supply Chain Trends in 2025
Effective planning sets the foundation for a well-oiled supply chain that can adapt to changes quickly.
 Article: What Is Supply Chain Forecasting? Let’s Break It Down
 Article: What Is Demand Forecasting? A Comprehensive Guide
2. Procurement
Procurement, or sourcing, is the process of obtaining the raw materials, components, or finished goods needed for your operations. A strong procurement strategy balances affordability with quality and ensures that your supply chain remains sustainable and ethical. This stage involves several steps:
 Finding reliable suppliers
 Negotiating contracts
 Fostering good supplier relationships
 Making sure the materials or goods meet quality and quantity requirements
 Tracking orders
 Monitoring costs
Your business will rely heavily on suppliers, so it’s essential to choose partners who communicate promptly and meet your agreed-upon standards of timeliness and quality.
But even the best partners may fall short, sometimes through no fault of their own.
To avoid the pitfalls that come with over-reliance on a single source, diversify your supplier base. Choose backup suppliers that you can turn to if a main partner faces delays, and consider choosing partners located close to your facilities to avoid the problems that can come with long transportation routes.
 Podcast episode: Building Better Supplier Relationships With Jill Barron From Summit Advisory Team
 Article: Building Relationships With Suppliers—The Right Way
3. Manufacturing
During the manufacturing phase, raw materials or components are transformed into finished products. This step of the supply chain process covers everything from production planning and scheduling to quality control and assembly.
Efficient manufacturing relies on clearly defined workflows, skilled labor (which requires consistent employee training), and the right technology.
One of the most crucial considerations during manufacturing is quality assurance; you should implement strict standards to ensure that every product meets customer expectations. By conducting regular quality inspections and testing at various points in the production cycle, you’ll reduce waste, improve productivity, and maintain a consistent standard of excellence.
4. Inventory management
Inventory management involves overseeing the storage, movement, and tracking of goods throughout your supply chain. This includes managing raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP) inventory, and finished products. The goal of inventory management is to strike a balance: keeping enough stock on hand to meet demand without overstocking.
To get inventory management right, consider investing in inventory management software or integrating this functionality into your warehouse management system (WMS).
 Podcast episode: Ecommerce Inventory and Labor Challenges With Steve Syverson From NFI eCom
 Article: Inventory Management: Benefits, Process, and Best Methods
5. Distribution
Distribution focuses on getting finished goods into the hands of consumers, either through in-store shopping or online ordering. As the touchpoint between your business and buyers, it’s one of the most important steps in the supply chain process.
Whether you’re delivering directly to customers or shipping to retail locations, the goal of distribution is to move products quickly, reliably, and cost-effectively.
When shipping to customers, you have a variety of order fulfillment strategies to choose from:
 In-house. With in-house fulfillment, you keep logistics functions within your business, allowing you to retain full control over storage, picking, packing, shipping, and returns.
 Third-party. When you partner with a third-party fulfillment provider, or 3PL, you outsource order fulfillment to them. Your products are stored in their distribution centers, and 3PL employees take care of picking, packing, and shipping.
 Dropshipping. In a dropshipping model, your suppliers ship orders directly to customers. This eliminates the need for your business to store inventory and handle fulfillment tasks.
 Hybrid. A hybrid model combines in-house fulfillment, third-party fulfillment, and/or dropshipping.
To improve the efficiency of your distribution, consider diversifying your fulfillment network; for example, you might use regional warehouses or micro-fulfillment centers to reduce delivery times.
Additionally, make sure to optimize last-mile delivery with shipping tools that provide real-time tracking and updates to customers, improving transparency and trust.
 Podcast episode: Tips for Last-Mile Success With Nicholas Daniel-Richards From Packiyo
 Article: Understand Direct-to-Consumer (DTC) Fulfillment
6. Returns processing
Returns processing, or reverse logistics, is all about managing the flow of goods from the customer back to your business. It covers everything from receiving returned products and inspecting them to deciding whether they can be restocked, refurbished, or recycled.
While returns might feel like an unnecessary hassle, remember that a smooth returns process is essential for maintaining customer satisfaction. But it’s not just about keeping customers happy—a great returns management strategy also helps you recapture value from returned items.
 Podcast episode: The Future of Returns With Eric Wimer From Sway
 Article: 6 Ways To Improve Your Returns Management Process
Supply chain process example
What do supply chain processes look like in practice? Let’s look at a hypothetical example of a company that specializes in hiking and camping gear. As they prepare to launch a new lightweight, waterproof hiking backpack, here’s how the supply chain process might unfold:
1. Planning. The company starts by analyzing sales data, customer feedback, and market trends to forecast demand for their new hiking backpack. They plan to produce 10,000 units initially, taking into account seasonal spikes in hiking gear sales during spring and summer.
2. Procurement. The company sources waterproof fabric and lightweight aluminum frame components from two trusted suppliers. They negotiate contracts and place orders well in advance to avoid delays during the peak production season.
3. Manufacturing. Next, materials are sent to a manufacturing facility where the backpacks are assembled. Workers conduct quality control checks on every batch to make sure the backpacks are durable and waterproof.
4. Inventory management. Once produced, the backpacks are stored in the company’s regional warehouses, where inventory management software tracks stock levels in real time.
5. Distribution. When customers place orders online, the backpacks are picked, packed, and shipped from the closest distribution center to keep delivery times short. The company also sends large shipments to outdoor equipment stores across the country using freight services.
6. Returns processing. If a customer discovers that the backpack doesn’t meet their needs, they can initiate a return through a convenient online portal. Staff members inspect returned backpacks, deciding whether to restock, repair, or recycle them.
How to optimize your supply chain
Is your supply chain operating as efficiently as possible? By prioritizing resilience and sustainability—and using the right supply chain technology—you’ll reduce costs, improve customer satisfaction levels, and scale your business more quickly.
Focus on resilience
In general, global supply chains bounce back quickly from disruptions. But your company won’t be able to say the same unless you proactively take steps to increase supply chain resilience. Consider the following ideas:
 Diversify. Expand your supplier base, avoiding over-reliance on a single source for essential parts and materials.
 Plan ahead. Develop contingency plans for common disruptions, such as natural disasters, supplier delays, or transportation issues.
 Stock up. A just-in-time inventory management strategy can be risky during a time of economic uncertainty and geopolitical conflict. Consider maintaining safety stock so you’re not left scrambling if a supplier doesn’t deliver on time.
Prioritize sustainability
Proving that you take sustainability seriously will help you attract and retain customers, employees, and investors. And while the journey to eco-friendly practices can seem arduous, you have plenty of opportunities to go green at every stage of your supply chain.
Get started by sourcing eco-friendly materials, reducing waste, and optimizing transportation to lower carbon emissions. Partner with suppliers who share your commitment to sustainability, and make sure they adhere to ethical labor and environmental standards.
> “Sustainability has gone from being a nice-to-have, to now being a business imperative.” — Tom Raftery, 2025 Supply Chain Sustainability Trends
Leverage technology
Finally, optimizing your supply chain requires the right technology. As your business scales, make appropriate investments in tools like supply chain management software, demand forecasting systems, shipping platforms, analytics tools, and real-time tracking solutions to improve visibility and efficiency across every process.
Simplify your supply chain with EasyPost Enterprise
As the touchpoint between your business and buyers, order fulfillment is one of the most important steps in the supply chain process. To keep customers coming back, you need a shipping process that’s affordable, reliable, and timely—and that’s why major organizations have turned to EasyPost Enterprise.
EasyPost Enterprise offers a suite of solutions designed for high-performance shippers: a shipping platform, advanced analytics software, consulting services, and more.
Learn how EasyPost Enterprise can streamline your fulfillment process.

Platform
