Global Perspectives: How Supply Chain Management Impacts International Business
Written by  Sarah Shelley
Career Paths
Share
 Facebook
 Twitter
 Linkedin
 Email
Supply chain management (SCM) has become a central element in the success of international business, influencing everything from production efficiency to competitive advantage. In a global economy where goods, services, and raw materials cross borders continuously, effective SCM ensures that multinational corporations can meet demand, reduce costs, and maintain quality standards across varied markets. As businesses expand operations internationally, SCM integrates logistics, inventory management, procurement, and distribution processes, making it indispensable for seamless global operations.
For multinational corporations, the effectiveness of their supply chains has direct implications for their market positioning, customer satisfaction, and operational resilience. By managing intricate supplier relationships and adapting to regional regulatory standards, these companies can mitigate risks associated with geopolitical shifts, trade restrictions, and economic fluctuations. Read on to explore how evolving SCM strategies enable firms to not only address complex logistical challenges but also capture new opportunities in emerging markets — all while aligning with the demands of a rapidly changing global business environment.
The Essential Role of Supply Chain Management in International Business
International business supply chain management plays a pivotal part in enabling global businesses to maintain efficient, responsive operations across borders. From managing the flow of raw materials to overseeing the distribution of finished products, effective SCM helps multinational corporations balance cost efficiency, demand fluctuations, and quality control. Supply chain services international solutions provide customized support to manage cross-border logistics and supplier relationships.
Here’s how SCM bridges international markets with local needs as well as strengthens a company’s competitive edge:
Bridging Global Markets and Local Demand
One key benefit of SCM is its ability to seamlessly connect global markets with local customer demands. Effective SCM strategies support businesses in navigating complex logistics, aligning international suppliers with regional market requirements, and adapting to cultural preferences. Creating a responsive supply chain allows companies to fulfill local consumer expectations while optimizing their global resource allocation, thereby supporting both international reach and localized service excellence.
Enhancing Competitive Edge through Efficient Supply Chains
Efficient SCM provides multinational corporations with a substantial competitive advantage by minimizing delays, managing costs, and ensuring timely deliveries. A well-managed supply chain allows companies to operate leaner, respond quickly to market changes, and achieve better pricing flexibility. Through reducing inefficiencies and embracing technologies likeAI and data analytics, businesses strengthen their position against competitors — especially in fast-paced global markets where even minor supply chain improvements can lead to significant gains in market share and customer loyalty​.
Key Components of a Robust International Supply Chain
Efficient international supply chains rely on several critical components, each playing a unique role in supporting operational resilience and responsiveness across global markets. Two core areas within this framework are strategic sourcing, which involves managing global supplier networks, and logistics strategies, which streamline distribution across borders.
Strategic Sourcing and Global Supplier Networks
Strategic sourcing is the process of selecting suppliers based not only on cost but also on quality, reliability, and resilience. In today’s global economy, companies often establish relationships with multiple suppliers across various regions to reduce dependency on any single source, in turn protecting against potential disruptions like geopolitical tensions or supply shortages. This multi-supplier approach, or “supplier +1” strategy, ensures that businesses maintain a steady flow of essential materials and components, even in volatile market conditions​.
Additionally, strategic sourcing tends to include continuous monitoring and assessment of suppliers’ performance, enabling firms to adapt to shifting market demands and maintain high standards in product quality.
Building robust supplier networks also requires a focus on compliance, as international sourcing involves navigating diverse regulatory landscapes. Companies often rely on compliance management tools and regular audits to ensure suppliers meet both legal standards and internal benchmarks. This simultaneous focus on risk mitigation and regulatory alignment enables businesses to maintain operational continuity and uphold ethical standards across all markets.
Logistics and Distribution Strategies Across Borders
Efficient logistics and distribution are critical to moving products smoothly across international borders, minimizing delays, and controlling costs. Global businesses implement tailored logistics strategies that account for factors like transportation infrastructure, local regulations, and trade agreements. For example, establishing regional distribution centers can reduce lead times and allow companies to adapt quickly to local demand fluctuations while maintaining efficient stock levels. Logistics planning also generally incorporates technology such as tracking systems and data analytics to enhance visibility and streamline inventory management​.
Cross-border logistics includes managing customs and compliance procedures, too, which are crucial to avoiding delays at international checkpoints. Investing in specialized customs teams or partnerships with local logistics providers, businesses can ensure smoother transitions through regulatory checks and optimize last-mile delivery processes. This careful orchestration of logistics helps firms provide consistent service to diverse global markets, ultimately boosting customer satisfaction and competitive advantage.
Overcoming Challenges in International Supply Chain Management
In the context of global operations, international business supply chain management is frequently complicated by varying regulatory frameworks, political risks, and the ever-present potential for disruptions. To navigate these challenges, companies must adopt adaptive strategies for regulatory compliance and risk mitigation that support seamless international operations and safeguard against external threats.
Navigating Regulatory Compliance and International Trade Laws
Navigating regulatory compliance requires companies to adhere to a diverse set of international trade laws that dictate how goods and services are moved across borders. These regulations vary widely by country and often require dedicated compliance programs to avoid legal repercussions, supply delays, or financial penalties.
For instance, organizations must comply with trade agreements, such as those outlined by the World Trade Organization (WTO), which set the groundwork for lawful international trade and minimize regulatory conflicts among countries. In addition, regional agreements like NAFTA or the EU’s Single Market harmonize regulations within specific areas, simplifying compliance for multinational corporations operating within those regions. Ensuring compliance with these laws helps companies avoid sanctions, protect brand reputation, and maintain uninterrupted operations.
To handle compliance effectively, businesses typically implement detailed compliance management systems (CMS) that include policies, training, and continuous risk assessments. This may involve screening suppliers, conducting due diligence, and aligning with local labor, environmental, and financial regulations. Staying informed on updates and legal changes is essential; even minor policy shifts can impact global supply chains significantly. Through integrating compliance monitoring into their supply chains, companies avoid legal issues as well as foster sustainable practices across their networks.
Mitigating Risks: From Political Instability to Supply Disruptions
Global supply chains are inherently vulnerable to risks stemming from political instability, economic shifts, and supply disruptions. For example, trade disputes, geopolitical tensions, or government-imposed sanctions can disrupt cross-border supply lines — leading to increased costs, delays, or even a need to shift sourcing strategies entirely. To address these challenges, companies often adopt multi-sourcing or “supplier +1” strategies to reduce dependency on a single supplier or country. This diversification not only lowers risk but also enhances resilience by allowing companies to switch suppliers or production sites if disruptions occur in one location.
Additionally, proactive risk management in global supply chains involves the use of predictive technologies and real-time data monitoring, which enable businesses to detect and respond to risks swiftly. By investing in transparent supply chain systems that provide end-to-end visibility, companies can better prepare for potential issues and ensure continuity. These adaptive measures (combined with diversified sourcing) help organizations manage supply chain complexities effectively, ensuring they remain competitive and responsive even in volatile international markets.
Elevate Your Contributions in Supply Chain Management With a Degree From UC
Take the next step in your career with the University of the Cumberlands' online master’s in supply chain management. This degree program equips students with the advanced skills and global insights needed to navigate the complex, fast-evolving world of supply chain management. Whether you’re aiming to deepen your expertise or pivot to a new role in logistics, sourcing, or international trade, our program offers the knowledge and flexibility to fit your career goals.
Don’t wait to advance your future: Request more information or apply today to join a community dedicated to shaping leaders in international business supply chain management.

The Heart of Business Success: Understanding Supply Chain Management
, effective supply chain management is essential for any company's success
, effective supply chain management is essential for any company's success
Not so long ago, the average consumer was not even aware of the supply chain or the
impact that it had on their daily lives. It was assumed that the products they wanted
to purchase would be available at the retailers that they chose to frequent, and they
didn't have to think much more about it.
In recent years, disruptions to the supply chain have highlighted the importance of
an optimized system. As a result, supply chain management professionals are beginning
to explore how advanced analytics and machine learning can help improve the supply
chain, making AI in supply chain management one of the top trending topics in the
field.
Why Supply Chain Management Matters Now More Than Ever
In a complex global economy, effective supply chain management is essential for any
company's success. Not only does an optimized supply chain allow a company to increase
its profits, but it's also the key to customer satisfaction, which is ultimately the
driving force behind lasting success. According to the Council of Supply Chain Management Professionals, effective supply chain planning and supply chain operations can:
The Rise of Global Supply Chains
The global economy means that all consumers and organizations are dependent on a global
supply chain, which is more interconnected and complex than ever before. One small
disruption in one region of the world, perhaps due to a natural disaster or social
upheaval, can have a lasting ripple effect on the entire supply chain, resulting in
shortages, rising prices, and more.
For example, at the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, the supply chain came to a screeching
halt as the world put itself on pause. At the same time, there was intense demand
among consumers for essentials like toilet paper, and it was difficult for stores
to restock items. It took nearly three years for the supply chain to recover from the pandemic. During that tumultuous time, it
was not uncommon to see piles upon piles of shipping containers sitting at ports,
significant shipping delays, and empty shelves at storefronts.
The extreme disruption caused by the COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the need for advanced
supply chain analytics, with many professionals turning to AI in the supply chain to
optimize the process and streamline the supply chain overall.
The Growing Demand for Sustainability
Recognizing the impact that the supply chain can have on the local environment and
on climate change, an increasing number of consumers are prioritizing brands that
have implemented ethical and sustainable supply chain practices. Some of the emerging trends in supply chain sustainability include:
The Need for Resilience in the Face of Uncertainty
The COVID-19 pandemic was perhaps the biggest wake-up call for supply chain managers,
as they quickly recognized that the existing supply chain was not as resilient or
agile as they had once believed. While there is hope that the world will not face
another pandemic in the near future, other unexpected events, such as natural disasters
and political unrest, can significantly disrupt and disturb supply chains around the
world. Having risk mitigation strategies in place allows supply chain managers to
create a more resilient supply chain.
How Supply Chain Management Affects Businesses
Supply chain management, when done right, seems to be a process that exists in the
background. While it may seem invisible, it's actually the backbone of most business
operations, ensuring that customers receive the high-quality products that they want
to purchase in a reasonable amount of time.
Cost Optimization Beyond Procurement
While negotiating supplier prices is a primary responsibility of many supply chain
managers, cost optimization extends beyond the procurement stage. Advanced strategies
such as inventory optimization, lean manufacturing, and logistics efficiency are used
to reduce costs overall, allowing companies to increase profits and develop dynamic
pricing strategies for consumers.
Enhancing Customer Experience Through SCM
An optimized supply chain can also significantly improve the overall customer experience.
A well-managed supply chain will:
Recently, the famed footwear company Adidas decided to invest heavily in automated supply chain operations in an effort to optimize
production. As a result, it has reduced its dependence on outsourced manufacturing,
and the company is now able to ship its products to consumers faster. This has given
them the competitive edge in a market heavily dominated by international manufacturing
and impacted by the global supply chain.
Supply Chain as a Driver of Innovation
While the supply chain may work in the background to keep business operations running
smoothly, it can also be a major driver of innovation within a company. The work to
optimize the supply chain can bring together suppliers, manufacturers, and retailers,
which presents the organization with a unique opportunity to create innovative and
impactful solutions.
Supply Chain Management in the Digital Age
The age of the AI-driven supply chain has arrived, with machine learning, prescriptive
analytics, and descriptive analytics dominating the conversation about how to optimize
and revolutionize the supply chain. Advanced analytics in supply chain allow supply
chain management professionals to make more informed decisions, particularly when
it comes to creating a faster, more efficient, more reliable, and more resilient supply
chain.
The Power of Data Analytics
Advanced analytics are redefining the supply chain management landscaping, giving
supply chain managers the power to increase their accuracy and precision when making
plans and implementing strategies. Supply chain data analyticsallow supply chain managers to:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Supply Chain
AI in the supply chain will quickly become the norm, as automation begins to take
over many processes in the global economy. In the supply chain, AI has the power to
streamline processes, reduce errors in every part of the supply chain and improve
overall efficiency. AI technology is expected to grow and advance significantly in
the coming years, which means that budding professionals will need to know how to
leverage AI in supply chain management.
The Role of Emerging Technologies
Emerging technologies will play a crucial role in supply chain management in coming
years, particularly as supply chain managers work toward optimization and improved
efficiency. Robotic process automation and blockchain technology will help improve production, transparency, and traceability within the supply chain.
The Human Side of Supply Chain Management
There is a human element to supply chain management that should not be ignored. By
remembering the importance of collaboration and connection, and the driving force
behind any demand for products and services, supply chain managers can ensure that
they can optimize production without losing sight of the purpose and intent of the
company.
Building Strong Relationships With Suppliers
Supply chain managers spend a lot of time and energy procuring supplies from suppliers,
which means that they must have solid interpersonal skills. Collaboration and communication
are key to developing successful and lasting supplier relationships, but
trust is at the core of any professional partnership. Your suppliers must be able
to trust that you are going to hold up your end of the bargain and continue to move
the supply chain forward.
The Importance of Ethical Practices
Ethical practices have never been more important in supply chain management, particularly
as consumers become more aware of the supply chain and continue to choose brands according
to the practices that they implement. Some of the most crucial ethical issues in supply
chain management include implementing fair labor practices, developing responsible
sourcing strategies, and prioritizing environmental sustainability.
Discover the Role That Advanced Analytics Plays in Supply Chain Management at UofM Global
At UofM Global, we recognize that up-and-coming professionals will need to have an
advanced understanding of the role of AI in supply chain management, which is why
we have developed a future-forward curriculum for our Bachelor of Business Administration degree in Supply Chain Management. The rigorous curriculum for this degree program covers foundational SCM concepts
while also exploring the critical role that supply chain data analytics plays in today's
modern economy, making this an excellent degree program for those who want to embark
on a lasting and lucrative supply chain management career.
Request more information about our online degree programs today.

What is Supply Chain Management and why is it so crucial today?
Supply Chain Management (SCM) is the dynamic coordination of interconnected businesses that work together to deliver products and services to customers. It encompasses every stage—from sourcing raw materials to delivering the final product—streamlining processes to reduce costs, improve efficiency, and elevate customer satisfaction.
Often misunderstood as simply logistics, SCM is much broader. Logistics focuses on the movement of goods, while SCM integrates manufacturing operations, marketing, product design, and even finance. The Council of Supply Chain Management Professionals (CSCMP) defines SCM as the overarching strategy that provides cohesion across all these activities, making it essential to modern business.

The Goals and Benefits of Supply Chain Management
Effective SCM delivers measurable business benefits. Here’s how:
The primary goal of SCM is to deliver maximum value to customers while controlling costs. Achieving this requires strategic planning, aligning supply and demand, and fostering collaboration across departments.
Why SCM Matters Today
In today’s fast-paced global economy, SCM is the key to competitive advantage. Companies with efficient supply chains can respond quickly to changing market conditions and stay ahead of the curve. In addition, SCM serves as a backbone for economic growth, enabling trade and commerce across industries. Its role in sustainability has also grown, with companies using SCM practices to reduce their environmental impact.
Supply Chain Management and Sustainability
Modern SCM integrates sustainability principles that emphasize environmental, social, and economic responsibility. This approach minimizes negative impacts while increasing efficiency and resilience.
Technologies like AI, blockchain, and IoT are transforming sustainable SCM by improving visibility, enhancing traceability, and enabling smarter resource management. These innovations are essential for businesses aiming to thrive in a competitive and environmentally conscious world.
Emerging Trends and Innovations in SCM
The field of SCM is evolving rapidly. Here are some of the latest developments:
Professional Roles in Supply Chain Management
SCM encompasses a variety of roles, each of which is critical to the success of the system:
These professionals work together to improve efficiency, control costs, ensure quality, and meet organizational goals. While each role has a unique focus, together they drive supply chain effectiveness.
Two relevant roles in this area are the supplier performance manager and the supply chain manager, which are often confused.
The Supplier Performance Manager is primarily responsible for overseeing the performance of suppliers to ensure they meet contractual obligations and quality standards. Key responsibilities include:
The Supply Chain Manager, on the other hand, has a broader scope, encompassing the entire supply chain process from sourcing to delivery. Key responsibilities include:

Understanding Salaries in Supply Chain Management
Salaries in SCM vary depending on factors such as experience, education, and industry. In Europe, a Supply Chain Manager earns between €60,000 and €100,000 annually, while a Supplier Performance Manager earns €50,000 to €80,000. In the U.S., the average salary for a Supply Chain Manager is approximately $126,550 per year, highlighting the lucrative potential of this field.
The Value of a Master’s Degree in SCM
A Master’s degree in Supply Chain Management offers benefits, including
However, not all Master’s in Operations and Supply Chain Management have the same value.
To get the most out of your studies, choose a reputable business school, such as GBSB Global, which is an internationally recognized, globally accredited, award-winning institution that provides students with a solid academic foundation.
The program of your choice should include project-based learning and internships that allow you to apply theoretical knowledge in a real-world setting. This hands-on experience is critical to understanding the complexities of supply chain operations. GBSB Global’s one-year Master of Science (MSc) in Management with Operations and Supply Chain Management, offered in its campuses of Barcelona (Spain), Malta and online, has an innovative learning approach that goes beyond traditional classes and basic theory, using game simulations, case studies, hands-on interactive exercises and entrepreneurial projects in real-world scenarios to prepare students to work effectively in the global business environment.
Third, the business school of your choice must demonstrate a dynamic ability to adapt to industry changes and innovations. The master’s curriculum must cover emerging trends and technologies in the field, preparing you to adapt to the rapidly changing landscape of global supply chains. Ranked among the best business schools in Europe, recognized as a Microsoft Showcase School and a leader in digital education, GBSB Global is at the forefront of redefining higher education to meet the demands of Industry 4.0.
A Strategic Investment for the Future
In summary, Supply Chain Management is more than a business necessity; it’s a strategic tool for success in a globalized economy. Earning a Master’s degree, especially from an accredited institution like GBSB Global, can open up new opportunities, higher salaries, and the ability to lead in an increasingly complex field.

HomeBlogWhat Is Supply Chain Management? Defining SCM and Its Role in Business Operations
What Is Supply Chain Management? Defining SCM and Its Role in Business Operations
April 23, 2024
Image 2: two men shaking hands with supply chain symbols surrounding them
The supply chain refers to the stages involved in getting a product from raw materials to the customer. Each step in the process is part of the supply chain, including sourcing, manufacturing, shipping, storage, distribution, and delivery.
Supply chain management strategies aim to ensure that each step of the supply chain operates at peak efficiency. In this post, we will explore the key concepts of SCM, emerging technology trends, and the overall benefits of a well-functioning supply chain.
Why Is Supply Chain Management Important?
Effective supply chain management offers multiple benefits to your business. Having an effective supply chain is important because:1,2
   It helps businesses deliver products to their customers promptly
   It improves customer service, boosting customer loyalty and retention
   It can increase organizational resilience
   It can reduce costs for operations and warehousing
   It can improve cash flow and profit margins
   It can support risk management efforts
Each of the benefits is important in isolation; when combined, they can provide a company with a significant competitive advantage. For example, by optimizing your supply chain, you can make sure you always have enough product on hand to meet customer demand without carrying excess inventory. Improving customer satisfaction while reducing operating costs is a double win, improving the bottom line while also helping to create loyal customers.
Supply Chain Management Overview
The supply chain management process orchestrates the various supply chain activities that take a product from conception to delivery. This encompasses raw material sourcing, manufacturing processes, inventory management, and logistics management, including delivery to the end customer. SCM aims to streamline supply chain operations, enhance customer satisfaction, and optimize supply chain performance at each stage. Here, we delve into the major components and management goals critical for optimizing business outcomes.
Raw Material Sourcing
Every product's journey begins with the raw materials. Effective supply chain management (SCM) ensures a steady flow of these materials from trusted sources, demonstrating the significance of managing supplier relationships. The goal of supply chain professionals is to minimize supply chain risk by building a resilient and sustainable supply chain network. This involves strategies for integrated planning with supply chain partners and the execution of sound risk management practices.
Inventory Management
Following material sourcing, managing inventory levels against market demand is crucial. Inventory management includes the strategic oversight of raw materials, work-in-progress, and finished goods. The aim of good supply chain management is to balance inventory levels to meet market demand, avoiding excess inventory and stockouts. Operations managers utilize tools like enterprise resource planning (ERP) to maintain this balance, improving supply chain visibility and overall performance.
Manufacturing Processes and Efficiency
The core of SCM is transforming raw materials into finished goods while optimizing the manufacturing process to meet customer demand without compromising on quality. Innovative emerging technologies and strategies like Just-in-Time (JIT) are leveraged to boost production efficiency, reduce waste, and shorten production timelines. This stage requires a proactive approach towards continually enhancing operations to efficiently meet demand.
Distribution, Logistics, and Customer Satisfaction
The concluding phase of the supply chain involves delivering the product to the customer through an integrated network of distribution centers and logistics management systems. The objective is to lower transportation costs while ensuring prompt delivery, thereby directly influencing customer satisfaction. Supply chain managers focus on integrated supply chain models that include reverse logistics for returns, augmenting the customer value proposition and fostering long-term customer relationships.
By strategically managing each element of the supply chain, businesses aim to reduce costs, efficiently meet demand, and ensure customer satisfaction, leading to improved supply chain performance and business growth. This overview underscores the dynamic, adaptive nature of SCM, requiring continuous evolution and strategic foresight from supply chain managers.
Key Concepts in Managing Supply Chains
Understanding the pivotal elements of supply chain management is essential in today’s competitive business environment. These are some critical areas central to effective supply chain management.
Supplier Relationship Management
Optimizing supplier relationships is at the heart of efficient supply chains. Good supplier relations, characterized by real-time communication and trust, lead to the streamlining of procurement processes, thus reducing lead times and enhancing the supply chain's overall responsiveness to market changes. Integrating Internet of Things (IoT) technology enhances the ability to manage supplier relationships by offering real-time insights into supplier performance, improving compliance, and encouraging collaborative improvements, thus ensuring procurement efficiency.3
Demand Forecasting
Accurate demand planning is crucial, allowing businesses to anticipate customer requirements adeptly. It combines industry-specific methodologies and analytic insights, improving strategic decisions and inventory management. Within lean management systems, the importance of precise forecasting cannot be overstated, as it ensures resources are allocated efficiently, reducing waste and enhancing responsiveness to customer demand fluctuations.4
Visibility
Supply chain visibility is crucial for managing operations efficiently. IoT technology enables businesses to monitor inventory accurately, predict demand variations and optimize stock levels to minimize shortages and excesses. Moreover, enhanced visibility across the supply network is paramount to successful environmental, social and governance (ESG) initiatives and vital for mitigating risks and safeguarding brand reputation against potential legal and social implications.5
Cost Reduction with Lean Systems
Lean systems, like Just-In-Time (JIT) inventory management, emphasize minimizing waste and enhancing the production process efficiency, leading to significant cost reduction and streamlined operations.6
Risk Management
Effective risk management, with a focus on agility, enables organizations to respond swiftly to disruptions. Agile supply chain strategies bolster resilience, ensuring businesses navigate challenges flexibly and maintain operational continuity.7
Incorporating such key concepts into supply chain management signifies leveraging critical techniques and tools that streamline operations, adeptly address current and future challenges, and ensure sustainable competitive advantage.
Emerging Technologies and Trends in Supply Chain Management
Technology advancements are increasingly influencing supply chain management, marking a new era of innovation and efficiency. These developments promise substantial improvements in agility, transparency, and productivity. Yet, they also present risks and challenges, notably around deployment and the requisite adaptation to harness their full potential effectively.
Cloud-Based Technology
Cloud-based technology is fundamentally altering supply chain processes, offering scalable, flexible solutions that significantly reduce the need for extensive physical infrastructure. This technology facilitates enhanced collaboration and agility within supply chain operations, enabling real-time decision-making and rapid adjustments to supply chain strategies.8
The Internet of Things
The deployment of the Internet of Things (IoT) has vastly improved supply chain transparency, allowing for the seamless tracking and monitoring of goods and assets across the globe. By harnessing real-time data, companies can boost operational efficiency, reduce costs, and proactively manage inventory levels to meet fluctuating demands.5
Robotics and Machine Clients
Advances in robotics and machine clients are driving unprecedented automation within supply chains. From automated warehouses to predictive analytics for stock management, these technologies are enabling more precise, efficient operations and transforming how businesses approach logistics and inventory management.3
AI Applications
AI applications have a profound impact on supply chain management, especially in analytics functions. By harnessing AI, businesses can unlock advanced forecasting capabilities, improve supplier performance analysis, and identify potential efficiency gains, thereby creating resilient, data-informed supply chains.9
Concerns About Implementation
While these technologies offer significant advantages, their implementation is not without challenges. Concerns related to data security, integration complexity, and the substantial investment required pose potential barriers. Additionally, there is an ongoing need to balance technological advancements with human factors, such as workflow changes and personnel training.
Best Practices for Successful Technology Integrations
A standardized approach to technology adoption is crucial to effectively navigating these challenges. Establishing clear processes and protocols ensures consistency and reliability. Equally important is investing in human capital through training and support, preparing the workforce to leverage new technologies to their full potential.
Case Study: Amazon's Technological Revolution in Supply Chain Management
At the heart of Amazon's supply chain strategy lies an adept integration of cloud computing, robotics, machine learning, and artificial intelligence. This fusion not only streamlines operations but also ensures cost efficiency across its global distribution network.10
Automation and Efficiency
Fulfillment by Amazon (FBA) illustrates how Amazon employs automation to optimize fulfillment processes. In its handling of storage, shipping, and customer service for merchants, FBA exemplifies the strategic application of machine learning and analytics functions, which enables businesses to concentrate on expansion while entrusting logistics to Amazon's technological prowess.10
Customer Satisfaction and Loyalty
Amazon Prime enhances the customer experience by rewarding subscribers with perks like free two-day shipping. This initiative not only boosts customer loyalty but also encourages a higher frequency of orders, showcasing the effective use of technology to drive sales and satisfaction.10
Data-Driven Optimization
Powered by Amazon Web Services (AWS), the company's supply chain benefits from unparalleled [visibility] and analytical capabilities. AWS's [cloud computing] services allow for efficient inventory management and customer behavior analysis, optimizing the supply chain's performance.10
Overcoming Challenges Through Innovation
Despite the impressive advancements, Amazon's journey is marked by challenges such as inventory management and guaranteeing product quality. Yet, Amazon's commitment to technological innovation and strategic implementation provides a roadmap for overcoming these obstacles, ensuring a seamless customer experience.10
This case study highlights the instrumental role emerging technologies play in enhancing supply chain agility, reducing costs, and prioritizing the customer's needs. Amazon's approach serves as a benchmark for operational excellence in the modern retail landscape.
Master Modern Business Processes with Seattle University
Supply chain management offers an exciting opportunity to help businesses across industries. Managing modern supply chains is just one of the skills you can develop in theonline MBA from Seattle University's Albers School of Business and Economics.
The values-centered program will help you craft a sustainable leadership career in today's dynamic business environment. The Albers online MBA is designed for working professionals, with forward-thinking coursework in decision-making, international management, operations and logistics, marketing, and more. Contact anadmissions outreach advisor to learn more about how the tech-connected online MBA helps you grow your professional network and your business acumen.

Understanding Supply Chain Management (SCM) and Its Importance
By
Jason Fernando
Image 1:max_bytes(150000):strip_icc():format(webp)/jason_mugshot__jason_fernando-5bfc261946e0fb00260a1cea.jpg)
Full Bio
   
Jason Fernando is a professional investor and writer who enjoys tackling and communicating complex business and financial problems. 
Learn about our editorial policies
Updated March 15, 2026
Reviewed byMargaret James
Fact checked by
Ryan Eichler
Image 3: Headshot:max_bytes(150000):strip_icc():format(webp)/InvestopediaHeadShot-988693bb87b54bb095bd3789cd117a50.jpg)
Fact checked by Ryan Eichler
Full Bio
   
Ryan Eichler holds a B.S.B.A with a concentration in Finance from Boston University. He has held positions in, and has deep experience with, expense auditing, personal finance, real estate, as well as fact checking & editing. 
Learn about our editorial policies
Image 5
Definition
Supply chain management (SCM) oversees the movement of goods and materials from suppliers to customers.
Key Takeaways
   Supply chain management (SCM) coordinates the production and distribution of goods to improve efficiency and reduce costs.
   Effective SCM manages the flow of materials, information, and products from suppliers to customers.
   Strong supply chains improve product quality, customer service, and inventory management.
   Companies may use different SCM models, such as agile or efficient models, depending on demand and industry needs.
   Modern SCM increasingly incorporates ethical and environmental considerations.
Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise.
ASK
What Is Supply Chain Management (SCM)?
Supply chain management (SCM) monitors and optimizes production and distribution to enhance efficiency and value. Effective SCM, by coordinating various processes from raw materials to the finished product, aims to reduce waste, maximize customer satisfaction, and secure a competitive edge.
Image 6: Supply Chain Management (SCM) Definition:max_bytes(150000):strip_icc():format(webp)/scm.asp-ADD-SOURCE-08b7e84e41a34ce18703eaadf947e055.png)
Investopedia / Alex Dos Diaz
Understanding the Mechanics of Supply Chain Management
SCM is a continuous effort by companies to make their supply chains efficient and economical.
Typically, SCM attempts to centrally control or link the production, shipment, and distribution of a product. Managing the supply chain helps companies cut costs, eliminate unnecessary steps, and deliver products faster. This is done by keeping tighter control of internal inventories, internal production, distribution, sales, and the inventories of company vendors.
SCM is based on the idea that nearly every product that comes to market does so as the result of efforts by multiple organizations that make up a supply chain. Although supply chains have existed for ages, a lot of companies didn't pay attention to them as a value-add to their operations until recently.
Open a New Bank Account
Advertiser Disclosure 
× 
The offers that appear in this table are from partnerships from which Investopedia receives compensation. This compensation may impact how and where listings appear. Investopedia does not include all offers available in the marketplace.
The Five Critical Phases of Supply Chain Management
A supply chain manager's role involves more than traditional logistics and purchasing; they must enhance efficiency, reduce costs, avoid shortages, and prepare for unexpected issues. Typically, the SCM process consists of these five phases:
1. Planning
To get the best results from SCM, the process usually begins with planning to match supply with customer and manufacturing demands. Companies must try to predict what their future needs will be and act accordingly. This involves considering the raw materials needed, equipment capacity, and staffing for each manufacturing stage.
Large businesses often rely on enterprise resource planning (ERP) software to help coordinate the process.
2. Sourcing
Effective SCM processes rely very heavily on strong relationships with suppliers. Sourcing entails working with vendors to supply the materials needed throughout the manufacturing process. Sourcing requirements vary across different industries. In general, SCM sourcing involves ensuring that:
   The raw materials or components meet the manufacturing specifications needed for the production of the goods.
   The prices paid to the vendor are in line with market expectations.
   The vendor has the flexibility to deliver emergency materials due to unforeseen events.
   The vendor has a proven record of delivering goods on time and of good quality.
SCM is especially critical when manufacturers are working with perishable goods.
Important
When sourcing goods, companies should be mindful of lead times and how well equipped a supplier is to meet their needs.
3. Manufacturing
Using machinery and labor to transform the raw materials or components the company has received from its suppliers into something new is the heart of the supply chain management process. This final product is the ultimate goal of the manufacturing process, though it is not the final stage of SCM.
The manufacturing process includes sub-tasks like assembly, testing, inspection, and packaging. During the manufacturing process, companies must be mindful of waste or other factors that may cause deviations from their original plans. For example, if a company is using more raw materials than planned and sourced for due to inadequate employee training, it must rectify the issue or revisit the earlier stages in SCM.
4. Delivery
After finalizing sales, companies must deliver products to customers. A company with effective SCM will have robust logistic capabilities and delivery channels to ensure timely, safe, and inexpensive delivery of its products.
This involves having backup or diverse distribution methods in case one transportation method fails. For example, how might a company's delivery process be impacted by record snowfall in distribution center areas?
5. Returns
The SCM process concludes with support for the product and customer returns.
The return process is often called reverse logistics, and the company must ensure it has the capabilities to receive returned products and correctly assign refunds for them. Whether a company is conducting a product recall or a customer is simply not satisfied with the product, the transaction with the customer must be remedied.
Returns offer valuable feedback, helping companies identify and rectify defective or poorly designed products. Without addressing the underlying cause of a customer return, the SCM process will have failed, and returns will likely persist into the future.
Different Models of Supply Chain Management
Supply chain management does not look the same for all companies. Each business has its own goals, constraints, and strengths that will shape its SCM process. These are some of the models a company can adopt to guide its SCM efforts:
Continuous Flow Model
The continuous flow model relies on a manufacturer producing the same good over and over and expecting customer demand will show little variation. One of the more traditional supply chain methods, this model is often best for mature industries.
Agile Model
The agile model prioritizes flexibility, as a company may have a specific need at any given moment and must be prepared to pivot accordingly. This method works best for companies with unpredictable demand or custom-order products.
Fast Model
This model emphasizes the quick turnover of a product with a short life cycle. Using a fast chain model, a company strives to capitalize on a trend, quickly produce goods, and ensure the product is fully sold before the trend ends.
Flexible Model
The flexible model works best for companies affected by seasonality. Some companies may have much higher demand requirements during peak season and low volume requirements in others. A flexible model of supply chain management ensures that production can easily be ramped up or wound down.
Efficient Model
Companies competing in industries with very tight profit margins may strive to get an advantage by making their supply chain management process the most efficient. That could involve coming up with ways to do a better job of utilizing equipment and machinery, managing inventory, and processing orders.
Custom Model
If any model above doesn't suit a company's needs, it can always apply a custom model. This is often necessary for highly specialized industries with high technical requirements, such as an automobile manufacturer.
Practical Example: How Walgreens Implements Supply Chain Management
Understanding the importance of SCM to its business, Walgreens Boots Alliance Inc. decided to transform its supply chain by investing in technology to streamline the entire process. That included using big data, collected from its 9,000 stores and 20,000 suppliers, to help improve its forecasting capabilities and better manage sales and inventory.1 In 2019, it appointed its first-ever chief supply chain officer.2
Walgreens Boots Alliance also incorporated SCM into its environmental, social, and governance (ESG) initiatives. For example, the company began asking suppliers to fill in an online survey that asks questions about their ESG practices, such as whether they have an emissions reduction target in place and the types of materials they use.3 4 More recently, the company has continued expanding its use of data analytics and automation to improve demand forecasting and supply chain efficiency.
Why Is Supply Chain Management Important?
Supply chain management is important because it can help achieve several business objectives. For instance, controlling manufacturing processes can improve product quality, reducing the risk of recalls and lawsuits while helping to build a strong consumer brand. At the same time, control over shipping procedures can improve customer service by avoiding costly shortages or periods of inventory oversupply. Overall, supply chain management provides multiple opportunities for companies to improve their profit margins and is especially important for businesses with large and international operations.
How Are Ethics and Supply Chain Management Related?
Ethics has become an increasingly important aspect of supply chain management, even leading to the establishment of a set of principles called supply chain ethics. Many investors today want to know how companies produce their products, treat their workforce, and protect the environment. As a result, companies respond by instituting measures to reduce waste, improve working conditions, and lessen their impact on the environment—all of which can involve SCM.
How Much Do Supply Chain Management Jobs Pay?
Supply chain managers across the United States earn average annual salaries ranging from $112,000 to $190,000 as of 2026, according to the website Glassdoor.5
The Bottom Line
Supply chain management (SCM) is essential for optimizing the flow of goods and services from raw materials to finished products. By effectively managing the stages of planning, sourcing, manufacturing, delivery, and returns, companies can cut excess costs, enhance product quality, and improve customer satisfaction. Whether utilizing a continuous flow, agile, or other SCM models, businesses must tailor their approach to meet specific industry demands and constraints. A robust SCM strategy not only minimizes inefficiencies and adds value at each stage but also positions companies for competitive advantage, potentially increasing revenue and reducing costs.

metadata.title: Understanding Supply Chain Management (SCM) and Its Importance

documents.page_content:
Unilog SC
Unilog SC
5 Biggest Challenges Facing Global Supply Chains Today

Organizations managing a global supply chain face a variety of unique challenges. Customers today expect efficient, on-time deliveries, and companies need a robust supply chain strategy to make it happen. Fluctuations in the global economy and changing political climates make the supply chain even more difficult to navigate.
At Unilog, we help our clients navigate the ever-changing global supply chain. As a 4PL provider, we provide end-to-end services, from inventory management to finding shipping partners. Our innovative Logivice software program serves as a control tower for your entire supply chain, compiling a broad range of data to help you stay on top of your orders.
Comprehensive supply chain support gives our clients the resources they need to tackle whatever challenges come their way. In this article, we’ll talk about some of the biggest challenges facing global supply chains today and what organizations can do to manage them.
Climate Change
Climate change is a problem that will have far-reaching effects for virtually every industry over the next few decades. Parts of the supply chain have already been affected as climate change has caused extreme weather patterns around the world.
For example, hurricanes have become much more intense in recent years, while floods and wildfires have become more frequent. Natural disasters like these can disrupt the entire global supply chain if they happen in an area with a large volume of warehouses or manufacturing facilities. In extreme weather situations, shipping trucks, boats, and planes won’t be safe to operate and therefore can’t run their regular routes.
Additionally, water levels have dropped in many rivers and other key waterways throughout the world due to draught. This has forced shipping companies to rethink their water routes. Seaports around the world are already heavily congested, and these global changes have made the problem worse.
These extreme weather events are very costly for suppliers to deal with. To safeguard your supply chain against unexpected weather events, consider working with multiple suppliers and shipping partners in different parts of the world to create redundancy. Another option is to work with suppliers in your own country or state when possible. While this won’t prevent extreme weather events, it gives you other options to prevent the entire supply chain from shutting down when they do happen.
Geopolitical Tensions
Tensions between key manufacturing countries throughout the world can also slow down the supply chain. For example, the Russo-Ukrainian War has forced many companies with operations in Europe and Asia to develop new shipping routes, which are often less efficient than their previous ones. Tensions between the US and China are another example of international politics affecting the supply chain.
Political tensions affecting the supply chain can range from wars, revolutions, and large-scale protests to sanctions, tariffs, and shipping restrictions. This is another situation where diversifying your suppliers can help you minimize shipping delays.
Labor and Material Shortages
Economic and cultural trends combined with unpredictable world events have resulted in damaging labor and material shortages. As COVID-19 spread throughout the world, workers who got sick typically had to take at least two weeks off of work, which limited production. Labor strikes and shortages have also become very common around the world as the workforce grapples with changes in working conditions and compensation.
Material shortages have also affected the global supply chain in recent years, which has had a domino effect on other industries. For example, the construction industry has been affected by shortages of lumber, steel, and other key materials. A shortage of semiconductor chips has also had a huge impact on many different industries, including consumer electronics and automobiles.
Both of these shortages have made it difficult for organizations to stick to their manufacturing and shipping timelines. Building a stockpile of inventory insulates your business against material shortages. Many companies now use automated technology to predict demand levels based on past consumer patterns, which you can use to build an appropriately-sized stockpile. Using alternative materials when possible can also help you keep your supply chain running in an emergency, although it may not be a viable long-term solution.
Managing a labor shortage is a more difficult challenge. There are a variety of things to consider when it comes to attracting the right team. Are your compensation packages appropriate for your local labor market? How do your working culture and conditions compare to your competitors? When choosing suppliers and shipping partners, it’s important to make sure you’re aligned on hiring strategies and that they know how to keep their positions staffed consistently.
Inflation
Inflation has become a major economic concern over the past year, affecting the supply chain in a variety of ways. Manufacturing and shipping costs have risen, forcing companies to rethink their budgets. Many organizations pass these increased costs on to their customers, but this can backfire. Inflation and cost-of-living increases mean that consumer spending power has diminished, which can result in reduced demand in some cases. Navigating inflation requires thoughtful budgeting to cover increased costs without pricing out your customer base. At Unilog, we help our clients find cost-efficient supply chain solutions to balance out increased expenses.
Congested Port Systems
Many of these supply chain problems have led to congested ports in some of the world’s largest cities. During the COVID-19 pandemic, many consumers started ordering their essentials online, causing shipping volumes to spike. Over two years later, shipping volumes are still very high, and many ports don’t have the infrastructure to manage it. Extreme weather and political conflicts around the world have forced suppliers to adjust their shipping routes, which has made congestion even worse in some areas.
These global challenges are will continue to affect organizations across many different industries. To keep your supply chain running smoothly, it’s important to be aware of these ongoing challenges and continually adjust your supply chain strategy. Working with a 4PL provider like Unilog takes time-consuming supply chain management tasks off your hands. We optimize your supply chain to keep your shipments running on time despite changes in the global economy and political landscape. 7 Challenges Facing Global Supply Chains
Learn how to overcome supply chain challenges by building resilient, adaptable ecosystems to evolve, optimize, and pivot in real time.
Table of Content
What are the Challenges Facing Supply Chain Management in 2026?
It’s never exactly been easy to manage a supply chain.
There’s always a possibility that something might go sideways. A war suddenly breaks out. A massive earthquake wipes out a supplier’s entire inventory. Trade relations between global powers break down over some behind-the-scenes disagreement.
But, in the past, there were some things that you could count on. For starters, lessons from the past were often a helpful guide for navigating present challenges.
Now, we find ourselves in an environment defined by disruption and uncertainty.
Supply chain leaders face a growing list of complex problems — none of which have a clear solution. There’s also no roadmap, because, at the risk of being cliché, these are “unprecedented times.”
It’s tempting to give up and let your business sink in the overwhelming chaos. Yes, the challenges are real — and yes, they’re also crazy complex. But, here’s the thing: there are solutions.
Below, we examine seven critical supply chain challenges and some of the ways that software can address them.
Image asking "Want more insights? Download our Guide to AI in the Supply Chain"
Image asking "Want more insights? Download our Guide to AI in the Supply Chain"
1. Data Management and Visibility Gaps
Almost every challenge on this list stems from the same root cause: inaccurate or incomplete data.
According to EY, limited visibility into supply chain functions remains a top challenge for businesses.
Research from Tive found that even as technology improves, visibility challenges persist. The report found that overall, first- and last-mile visibility has improved. Yet 37% of companies still struggle to track activity during the mid-mile stage of transportation.
This is a problem because visibility is the first step toward building resilient and agile supply chains. 
Leaders struggle to answer basic questions in real-time: Where is the inventory right now? Which orders are at risk? How are key suppliers performing this week, not last quarter?
You also can’t respond to time-sensitive issues, such as incoming cyber threats or safety recalls. And, you’ll most likely miss the red flags and anomalies that warn of the next big “black swan” disruption.
The core data-management challenges include:
With the right software, organizations can overcome this challenge.
Migrating to a cloud ERP like Microsoft Dynamics 365 enables you to combine your entire business and supply chain — finance, procurement, inventory, warehousing, logistics, etc.— in one location.
Modern data platforms – i.e., data lakes, lakehouses, and data management solutions – are designed to tackle challenges related to controlling and operationalizing supply chain data. Microsoft Fabric unifies data from ERP, CRM, WMS, TMS, and partner systems into a single analytics-ready layer.
You can then use tools like Power BI, embedded dashboards, Copilot, and digital supply chain control towers to provide planners and executives with a shared, real-time view of operations.
For supply chain teams, this foundation sets the stage for everything else. Better risk management. Fewer security risks. More accurate planning. The ability to leverage intelligent automation and AI agents.
2. Tariffs & Geopolitical Tensions
Geopolitical and trade risk have moved from background noise to a daily operating constraint.
A recent DP World study found that 82% of supply chain leaders view geopolitical disruption as a serious risk. Yet, only about a quarter feel adequately prepared.
Conflicts, sanctions, export controls, and shifting alliances are forcing companies to redraw trade maps and rethink dependencies on specific countries, corridors, or ports.
For supply chain teams, that means more frequent changes to tariffs and duties, unexpected route closures, and pressure from boards and regulators to reduce exposure to high-risk regions. Long, lean, single-sourced networks built purely for cost are now a liability.
This creates three key problems:
Cloud-based ERP and supply chain planning platforms help by consolidating supplier, demand, logistics, and cost data in a single location.
Layering analytics and AI on top enables planners to run scenarios, compare options, and receive alerts when conditions exceed a threshold. So you’re not finding out about trade shocks in the news after the damage is done.
You can see the impact of this capability in industries hit directly by tariff volatility.
In 2025, growers faced rapid swings in fertilizer tariffs and availability.
For our clients using SilverLeaf, a solution that integrates core processes for cannabis and greenhouse growers with Dynamics 365 Business Central, real-time data enables rapid response.
Instead of scrambling when prices surged or materials got stuck at the border, unified data enabled those clients to take action.
They were able to model cost impacts in real-time, adjust purchasing strategies, and protect planting timelines when shipments were delayed at customs. They quickly reworked budgets, rerouted orders, and were able to maintain production schedules.
The lesson applies across industries. When tariffs change faster than your spreadsheets, you need systems that provide immediate, actionable guidance.
3. Inflation & Economic Pressure
Inflation and economic uncertainty remain near the top of the list of impactful supply chain trends.
Among those surveyed in the 2025 MHI Annual Industry Report, 38% ranked inflation as the most significant force shaping supply chains. And, another 37% chose economic uncertainty.
For most organizations, that pressure shows up as:
The challenge is that many companies still lack a clear link between operational decisions and financial impact. Procurement negotiates contracts. Logistics reroutes shipments. Planners adjust inventory buffers.
But without connected data, it’s hard to understand how those decisions affect margin, cash flow, and customer commitments.
Modern ERP platforms that integrate all supply chain functions with finance systems change that equation.
They allow you to:
Instead of reacting to cost spikes months later, you can see trends in near real time and course-correct before they show up in your P&L.
4. Cybersecurity
Supply chains have become one of the most attractive targets for cybercriminals.
As supply chains become more digitalized, exposure to cyber threats increases.
Attackers often go after suppliers, logistics providers, and software vendors, because they see them as “weak links” that can provide backdoor entry into more valuable targets.
WTW’s 2025 Global Supply Chain Risk analysis found that cybersecurity has jumped sharply as a core concern among supply chain leaders. The report also noted that a growing share of reported attacks now originates from third parties.
In October 2025, The Guardian reported that about a third of business leaders had seen an increase in cyberattacks on their supply chains in the last six months alone. These incidents triggered a range of issues, including logistics disruptions, factory shutdowns, and losses exceeding tens of millions of dollars.
Much of the problem stems from poor visibility. Though, naturally, legacy systems make matters worse.
For example, older ERPs and custom logistics solutions tend to lack modern identity and access controls, universal monitoring and threat detection, and robust event logging. Systems often can’t be patched quickly, requiring IT to perform manual updates.
When vulnerable systems connect to partner networks via VPNs or flat network connections, the results can be devastating. One compromised account or exposed endpoint can grant access to attackers, who can then achieve broad lateral movement.
Addressing this risk requires both governance and technology:
Cloud ERP and supply chain platforms backed by enterprise-grade security tooling can help you get ahead of this challenge.
Integrated SIEM, XDR, and identity & access protection solutions make it easier to harden your environment, automate patching, and gain real-time insight into suspicious activity.
Meanwhile, automatic updates and AI-enhanced workflows proactively safeguard against threats, without relying on overstretched IT teams.
5. Sustainability & ESG Compliance
Sustainability has shifted from a marketing talking point to hard requirement.
Regulations like the EU’s Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and various supply chain due diligence laws require granular data on emissions, human rights, and traceability across complex, multi-tiered networks.
Non-compliance, of course, carries reputational, legal, and financial consequences.
Sustainability has become a greater concern for customers and investors, who now expect more than “green-washed” climate pledges and a few stats about emissions. They’re looking for more transparency into aspects such as water use, labor practices, and climate adaptation plans.
The operational challenges:
Modern supply chains and sustainability platforms are converging to address this. By integrating ESG data into your operational systems, you can:
The upside is that many sustainability initiatives also support traditional supply chain and business objectives. For example, waste reduction, energy efficiency, and optimized route planning reduce costs and minimize risks.
The point is, in a unified system, compliance is no longer a burden, but an opportunity to create a competitive advantage.
6. Realizing ROI on AI
Many organizations have experimented with AI for forecasting, routing, or quality. But they still struggle to scale those efforts or tie them to clear business outcomes.
Part of the problem is that supply chain orgs are approaching AI as a collection of one-off side projects. Another issue is data connectivity. Research from SAP and MIT has emphasized that AI can only deliver value when it has fast, consistent access to high-quality data across the enterprise.
The main challenges:
Unlocking its true potential means leaders must embrace more holistic AI strategies. This also means that AI must be embedded across all core platforms and processes.
That looks like:
When those tools sit on top of a unified data foundation, it’s easier to connect AI initiatives to supply chain outcomes and KPIs like forecast accuracy, inventory turns, on-time in-full (OTIF), and cost-to-serve.
7. Labor Shortages and Skills Gaps
Labor and skills shortages remain one of the biggest challenges facing supply chains.
The MHI report we mentioned found that workforce and talent shortages were cited by 35% of respondents as a top concern, second only to inflation and economic uncertainty.
The gap is twofold:
These issues make it difficult to scale operations, adopt new tools, or manage complex supply chain operations.
There’s also the added challenge of trying to “transform” when staff are already stretched thin trying to keep up with existing responsibilities. That, in turn, means tech initiatives are more likely to stall out – further wasting resources and making the business even more vulnerable to risk.
Software can’t solve the talent shortage by itself (and, no, replacing people with AI is not the answer, either). But the right technology can help your existing workforce become more effective.
And, it can help you create an environment that makes it easier to attract and retain the right people moving forward. A few examples:
Combined with thoughtful workforce and change management strategies, as well as internal upskilling programs, these technologies can help you build a team capable of running a digitally enabled, data-driven supply chain.
Final Thoughts
In 2026, the biggest supply chain challenges — geopolitics, inflation, cyber risk, AI adoption, etc. — aren’t really separate problems. They’re different expressions of the same reality.
Supply chains are now dynamic, digital ecosystems that span continents, partners, and platforms.
To navigate that reality, organizations need:
Get those building blocks in place, and the challenges that feel overwhelming today become the strategic levers you can use to control costs, reduce risk, and win big tomorrow.
Struggling with visibility gaps, volatile costs, or fragile supplier networks? Velosio helps supply chain organizations replace outdated systems with modern, AI-powered ecosystems that deliver real-time insight and predictable ROI.
 Contact us today to find out how we can support your transformation.
Ready to take action?
Talk to us about how Velosio can help you realize business value faster with end-to-end solutions and cloud services.
